title: Neural Architectures as Functional Priors in Physics-Informed Control Problems
comment: 17 pages, 6 figures. Physics-informed neural networks, optimal control, spectral bias, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
summary: In this work we investigate the role of neural architectures as implicit functional priors in control problems governed by ordinary differential equations. Rather than focusing on highly complex problems, our objective is to investigate architecture-dependent effects in controlled dynamical systems within the simplest physically interpretable settings possible. In particular, we study a controlled linear RLC electrical circuit and a nonlinear Duffing-type dynamical system. Both systems are analyzed first through classical optimal-control formulations and later through PINN-based approaches. We compare different combinations of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and Fourier-based KAN-like architectures, and analyze their influence on the resulting controls. The numerical experiments suggest that different architectural choices systematically generate qualitatively distinct controls, even under identical governing equations, loss functionals, initial and target states, training parameters and physical constraints. Significant differences appear in the spectral structure, smoothness, energy distribution, and phase-space behavior of the learned solutions. A central observation of this work is the emergence of a functional specialization phenomenon when the neural architectures are allowed sufficient freedom to shape the structure of the learned controls. More specifically, in the systems considered here, Fourier-based architectures tend to produce trajectories with richer oscillatory content, whereas smoother low-frequency-biased architectures tend to generate more regular and energetically efficient controls. This suggests that different functional components of the control problem may be handled more efficiently by different neural architectures, leading to an implicit specialization between state representation and control generation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how different neural architectures (MLPs vs Fourier-based KAN-like) act as implicit functional priors in optimal control of ODE-governed systems (RLC circuit and Duffing oscillator). Results show architectural choices systematically shape control solutions, with Fourier architectures producing oscillatory controls and smoother architectures yielding more regular, energy-efficient solutions, suggesting implicit functional specialization.
AI.motivation: To understand how neural architecture choices implicitly influence control solutions in dynamical systems, distinct from problem formulation or training conditions.
AI.method: Compare classical optimal-control formulations with PINN-based approaches using MLPs and Fourier-based KAN-like architectures on controlled linear RLC circuit and nonlinear Duffing system.
AI.result: Different architectures produce qualitatively distinct controls in spectral structure, smoothness, energy distribution, and phase-space behavior despite identical conditions, with Fourier architectures yielding oscillatory controls and low-frequency-biased architectures producing regular, efficient controls.
AI.conclusion: Neural architectures act as implicit functional priors leading to emergent functional specialization, where different architectures handle different control problem components more efficiently.

title: Moreau-Yosida-based Kohn-Sham Inversion for Periodic Systems
summary: Density-potential inversion for periodic systems within Moreau-Yosida-regularised density-functional theory is investigated, both theoretically and numerically. We develop the framework in a periodic homogeneous Sobolev space and use it to recover the exchange-correlation potential of Kohn-Sham theory through a limiting procedure. A key analytical ingredient is the proof of lower semicontinuity of the non-interacting kinetic-energy functional in the chosen topology. The proximal mapping, together with its algorithmic evaluation, plays a central role in the resulting inversion scheme. Numerical experiments illustrate the performance and properties of the method for both the Kohn-Sham and Gross-Pitaevskii equations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a density-potential inversion method for periodic systems using Moreau-Yosida regularized DFT, proving key analytical properties and demonstrating numerical performance on Kohn-Sham and Gross-Pitaevskii equations.
AI.motivation: To develop a theoretically rigorous and numerically viable framework for recovering exchange-correlation potentials in periodic Kohn-Sham systems through density-potential inversion.
AI.method: Uses Moreau-Yosida regularization within periodic homogeneous Sobolev spaces, proves lower semicontinuity of the non-interacting kinetic-energy functional, and employs proximal mapping for algorithmic inversion.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate successful performance of the inversion method for both Kohn-Sham and Gross-Pitaevskii equations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a sound theoretical and computational approach to density-potential inversion for periodic systems via Moreau-Yosida regularized DFT.

AI.motivation: To develop a data assimilation method that reduces computational complexity and cost compared to standard fully coupled approaches while maintaining comparable accuracy, by leveraging modular 2-step nudging combined with BDF2 time discretization.
AI.method: The method couples BDF2 time discretization (forecast step) with a modular 2-step nudging-based data assimilation (analysis step), where the analysis step can be made explicit using projection operator $I_H^2$, achieving implicit stability with lower complexity.

AI.conclusion: Modular BDF2 data assimilation offers a computationally efficient alternative to standard fully coupled methods without sacrificing accuracy, validated by both theoretical analysis and numerical experiments.

title: Bregman-projected mirror methods for regularized stationary mean-field games
summary: We develop and analyze a Bregman-projected mirror iteration for low-order regularizations of stationary mean-field game (MFG) systems in their natural Banach space setting. For separable Hamiltonians of the form \(H(x,p,m)=H_0(x,p)-g(m)\), with quadratic or super-quadratic Hamiltonian growth and linear or super-linear density couplings, we formulate a low-order \(\barγ\)-Laplacian regularization of the stationary MFG system as a variational inequality on \(L^{\barβ}(\mathbb T^d)\times W^{1,\barγ}(\mathbb T^d)\). To approximate solutions of this regularized variational inequality, we introduce a Bregman geometry matched to the mixed Lebesgue--Sobolev exponents of the problem and analyze a constrained two-step mirror method with frozen operator evaluation. For the exact constrained iteration and each fixed regularization parameter \(\epsi>0\), we derive a one-step Bregman inequality and use it to prove that the constrained iteration converges strongly to the unique solution of the regularized variational inequality under natural summability conditions on the step sizes. Numerical experiments on one- and two-dimensional models, validated against exact test solutions, illustrate residual decay under mesh refinement and suggest improved practical performance of the two-step implementation in the tested discretizations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a Bregman-projected mirror iteration for solving regularized stationary mean-field game (MFG) systems in Banach spaces, proving convergence and demonstrating numerical performance.
AI.motivation: To address the need for efficient numerical solvers for low-order regularized stationary MFG systems with separable Hamiltonians (quadratic/super-quadratic growth) and linear/super-linear density couplings, formulated as variational inequalities in mixed Lebesgue-Sobolev spaces.
AI.method: Proposes a Bregman geometry matched to mixed Lebesgue-Sobolev exponents, formulates a \(\bar\gamma\)-Laplacian regularized MFG system as a variational inequality on \(L^{\bar\beta}\times W^{1,\bar\gamma}\), and introduces a constrained two-step mirror method with frozen operator evaluation and Bregman inequality analysis.
AI.result: Derives a one-step Bregman inequality and proves strong convergence of the constrained iteration to the unique solution under natural summability conditions on step sizes; numerical experiments on 1D/2D models show residual decay under mesh refinement and improved practical performance of the two-step implementation.
AI.conclusion: The Bregman-projected mirror iteration provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective approach for solving regularized stationary MFG systems, with validated numerical performance.

title: Bayesian optimization of stellarator alpha-particle confinement using data-informed parameter spaces and dimensionality reduction
summary: Modern stellarators are typically designed by optimizing the shape of the plasma boundary surface, with the parameters taken to be Fourier amplitudes. Many promising optimization algorithms such as Bayesian methods require bound constraints on the parameters and are most efficient when each parameter is scaled similarly to the others. With the typical Fourier parameterization, it is unclear how to set these bounds: wide constraints lead to self-intersecting boundaries and frequent failures of the MHD equilibrium calculation, while tight bound constraints limit expressiveness. To address these issues, here we propose two new parameter spaces for stellarator optimization. Both begin with a dataset of existing stellarator boundaries. In the first approach, a quantile transformation is applied to each Fourier degree of freedom, mapping the data distribution to a uniform distribution on the unit interval. In the second approach, principal component analysis (PCA) is applied to points on the boundaries, followed by a quantile transformation. For both approaches, the transformed variables become the degrees of freedom, naturally bounded to [0, 1]. The PCA method has the additional benefit of dimensionality reduction, with high expressiveness for a small number of parameters. The methods are demonstrated via Bayesian optimization for good alpha-particle confinement with guiding-center tracing inside the optimization loop, using asynchronous parallelization. These optimizations yield stellarator configurations with excellent fast-particle confinement in fields that can be far from quasisymmetric or quasi-isodynamic.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two new parameter spaces for stellarator optimization—quantile transformation of Fourier degrees of freedom and PCA on boundary points followed by quantile transformation—to replace the traditional Fourier parameterization that suffers from self-intersecting boundaries and scalability issues. Both methods naturally bound parameters to [0,1] and are demonstrated via Bayesian optimization for alpha-particle confinement, achieving good results even in non-quasisymmetric configurations.
AI.motivation: Traditional Fourier parameterization for stellarator optimization lacks natural bound constraints—wide bounds cause self-intersecting boundaries and MHD equilibrium failures, while tight bounds limit expressiveness. Additionally, parameters are not similarly scaled, hampering the efficiency of algorithms like Bayesian optimization.
AI.method: Two parameter spaces are proposed: (1) Apply a quantile transformation to each Fourier degree of freedom, mapping the data distribution to a uniform distribution on [0,1]. (2) Apply PCA to points on existing stellarator boundaries, then apply a quantile transformation. Both approaches bound all degrees of freedom to [0,1] naturally.
AI.result: Demonstrated via Bayesian optimization for good alpha-particle confinement with guiding-center tracing, using asynchronous parallelization. The methods yield stellarator configurations with excellent fast-particle confinement, even in fields far from quasisymmetric or quasi-isodynamic.
AI.conclusion: The proposed parameter spaces address the bound constraint and scaling issues of traditional Fourier parameterization, enabling more effective stellarator optimization. The PCA method additionally offers dimensionality reduction with high expressiveness for few parameters.

title: sft-wick: A formalism and package for Feynman-diagram expansion and evaluation in stochastic field theories
comment: 32 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to Computer Physics Communications. The sft-wick package is open source and available at https://github.com/StatFieldTheory/sft-wick
summary: When stochastic field dynamics are cast into a path-integral formulation, perturbation theory becomes systematic but the resulting expansion quickly grows combinatorially large. The setting targeted here includes multi-component, multi-dimensional fields with matrix propagators, tensor-valued couplings, and non-Gaussian driving noise specified by arbitrary $n$-point cumulants. Wick pairings grow factorially, and component indices must be routed through the tensor-valued vertices. The useful output is not a raw contraction list, but a diagram table: one entry per topology, with multiplicities, coupling sums, signs, and causal constraints resolved. We present sft-wick, an open-source Python package that constructs these diagram tables and computes their integrals numerically. Given an action and an observable, it enumerates topologically distinct Feynman diagrams, derives their algebraic coefficients, and evaluates the resulting diagram integrals from user-supplied response and cumulant functions. The core algorithm enumerates spatial topologies before routing component indices, avoiding contraction-by-contraction Wick expansion. Response-field constraints, including vanishing response-response contractions, the ito prescription, and the absence of causal response loops, are enforced during enumeration. Predictions are validated against direct Langevin simulation, agreeing to within the simulation's statistical noise.

AI.tldr: sft-wick is an open-source Python package that automatically enumerates topologically distinct Feynman diagrams, computes their algebraic coefficients, and evaluates integrals for stochastic field dynamics, validated against Langevin simulations.
AI.motivation: Stochastic field dynamics in path-integral formulation suffer from combinatorially large expansions due to Wick contractions, tensor-index routing, and multi-component fields, making manual diagram generation impractical.
AI.method: The package enumerates spatial topologies before routing component indices (avoiding brute-force Wick expansion), enforces response-field constraints (e.g., no response-response contractions, causal loop absence), and produces diagram tables with multiplicities, coupling sums, signs, and causal constraints.
AI.result: Predictions from sft-wick agree with direct Langevin simulations within statistical noise, validating correctness.
AI.conclusion: sft-wick provides a systematic, automated tool for Feynman diagram generation in stochastic field theories, handling multi-component fields, tensor couplings, and non-Gaussian noise efficiently.

title: Regularity of the positional penalization function in inter-sign optimal transport on real measures
comment: Together with Bwo'nyahre et al. (2026), this completes a three-part framework for signed measure optimal transport: (1) existence, uniqueness, and fractal preservation; (2) local regularity, governing equations, and well-posedness; and (3) a variational characterization of dimensional distortion in signed fractal measures
summary: We study the Monge--Kantorovich optimal transport problem between two signed measures~$μ$ and~$ν$ on convex compact subsets of~$\mathbb{R}^d$, with a positional penalization function~$λ(x, y)$ that modulates the cost of inter-sign transport. Using four independent positive measures~$(π^{++}, π^{+-}, π^{-+}, π^{--})$ as decision variables, we prove that the admissible set~$\mathcal{A}(μ, ν)$ is weakly-$*$ compact and non-empty if and only if $μ^+(X) = ν^+(Y)$ and~$μ^-(X) = ν^-(Y)$. Strong duality is established via the Kantorovich minimax theorem, yielding a new compatibility condition on~$λ$ at the intersection of inter-sign supports. The penalization~$λ$ is shown to be Lipschitz and to admit Alexandrov second derivatives almost everywhere. Modified Monge--Ampère equations governing inter-sign transport maps are derived in the Alexandrov sense, with well-posedness characterized by $σ\det(D^2_{yx}Λ) e > 0$. The classical Brenier equation is recovered in the limit~$λ\to 0$.

AI.tldr: This paper extends optimal transport to signed measures using four independent positive measures, establishes strong duality via Kantorovich minimax theorem, derives modified Monge-Ampère equations for inter-sign transport, and recovers classical Brenier equation as penalization vanishes.
AI.motivation: To generalize Monge-Kantorovich optimal transport from probability measures to signed measures, enabling transport between measures with both positive and negative mass, which is relevant for applications like imaging, finance, and PDEs where signed data naturally arise.
AI.method: Introduces four independent positive measures (π^{++}, π^{+-}, π^{-+}, π^{--}) as decision variables; proves compactness and non-emptiness conditions; applies Kantorovich minimax theorem for strong duality; derives modified Monge-Ampère equations in Alexandrov sense.
AI.result: Admissible set is weakly-* compact and non-empty iff total positive and negative masses match separately; new compatibility condition on penalization λ established; λ is Lipschitz with Alexandrov second derivatives a.e.; well-posedness characterized by σ det(D²_{yx}Λ) e > 0; classical Brenier equation recovered as λ→0.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous framework for signed optimal transport with positional penalization, unifying classical results and extending theory to signed measures with well-posedness conditions.

title: On a class of modified Cayley--Magnus methods
summary: We introduce a new class of numerical integrators for the time integration of non-autonomous linear ordinary differential equations whose coefficient matrix is sparse and evolves within a quadratic matrix Lie group. In contrast to standard Lie group integrators, the proposed methods avoid the evaluation of matrix exponentials acting on vectors and instead rely on solving a sequence of linear systems with sparse coefficient matrices. Moreover, they are well suited for problems arising from unbounded operators, as they inherently produce bounded solutions. We construct optimised schemes of orders four and six and assess their performance on a representative numerical example, demonstrating clear advantages over existing Lie-group integrators.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces new numerical integrators for non-autonomous linear ODEs with sparse matrices in quadratic Lie groups, replacing expensive matrix exponentials with linear system solves, and demonstrates advantages of fourth- and sixth-order schemes.
AI.motivation: To develop numerical integrators that avoid costly matrix exponential evaluations for non-autonomous linear ODEs with sparse coefficient matrices evolving in quadratic matrix Lie groups, while handling unbounded operators and producing bounded solutions.
AI.method: Proposes integrators that solve sequences of linear systems with sparse coefficient matrices instead of evaluating matrix exponentials acting on vectors. Constructs optimized fourth- and sixth-order schemes specifically targeting quadratic matrix Lie groups.
AI.result: The proposed methods demonstrate clear advantages over existing Lie-group integrators on a representative numerical example, with optimized schemes of orders four and six showing improved performance.
AI.conclusion: The new class of integrators effectively handles non-autonomous linear ODEs with sparse matrices in quadratic Lie groups, offering computational advantages by avoiding matrix exponentials and naturally producing bounded solutions for unbounded operator problems.

title: Dephasingless laser wakefield acceleration in a plasma waveguide
summary: Laser wakefield accelerators (LWFAs) provide extremely large accelerating gradients for compact electron accelerators and photon sources but are limited by dephasing, where trapped electrons outrun the accelerating phase of the wakefield. Flying-focus pulses can eliminate dephasing by driving a wake at the vacuum speed of light, but these pulses involve tradeoffs such as varying spot size, long duration, or large plasma volume. Here we show that a spatiotemporally structured laser pulse propagating in a plasma waveguide can drive a wakefield at the vacuum speed of light while maintaining a constant spot size and ultrashort duration. The pulse is formed by superposing plasma-waveguide modes with appropriately selected frequencies. Compared with flying-focus approaches, the waveguide substantially reduces the required plasma volume. Scaling laws and quasi-3D particle-in-cell simulations show that the single-stage energy gain increases linearly with the number of modes used to construct the pulse, enabling larger energy gains or shorter stages than standard LWFA.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a spatiotemporally structured laser pulse in a plasma waveguide that can drive a wakefield at vacuum speed of light, eliminating dephasing issues in LWFAs while maintaining constant spot size and ultrashort duration, outperforming flying-focus approaches.
AI.motivation: LWFAs suffer from dephasing limitations where trapped electrons outrun the accelerating phase. Flying-focus pulses address this but introduce tradeoffs like varying spot size and large plasma volume.
AI.method: The pulse is formed by superposing plasma-waveguide modes with appropriately selected frequencies, maintaining constant spot size and ultrashort duration within a waveguide.
AI.result: Scaling laws and quasi-3D simulations show single-stage energy gain increases linearly with the number of modes used, enabling larger energy gains or shorter stages than standard LWFA.
AI.conclusion: The waveguide-based approach substantially reduces required plasma volume compared to flying-focus methods and eliminates dephasing while preserving pulse quality.

title: TorchNEP: Ultra-Efficient and Accurate Training of Neuroevolution Potentials
summary: Neuroevolution Potential (NEP) is one of the most efficient machine-learned interatomic potential frameworks for large-scale atomistic simulations. However, its original training strategy remains computationally demanding, limiting systematic exploration of model architectures and training protocols. Here, we present TorchNEP, a PyTorch-based implementation of NEP that combines analytically derived gradients, adaptive optimization, and a two-stage training strategy. TorchNEP accelerates training by more than two orders of magnitude while maintaining full compatibility with existing NEP models. We further show that the improvement in predictive accuracy primarily originates from the two-stage training protocol rather than the optimization algorithm itself. Across diverse benchmark datasets, TorchNEP consistently improves force and stress predictions while maintaining comparable or improved energy accuracy. Benchmark evaluations on elemental and alloy systems demonstrate enhanced predictive performance for both atomic configurations and key materials properties. Furthermore, we show that increasing model complexity does not necessarily improve predictive performance despite reducing training errors. Overall, TorchNEP provides an efficient and flexible training framework for developing more accurate and robust machine-learned interatomic potentials.

AI.tldr: TorchNEP is a PyTorch-based reimplementation of the Neuroevolution Potential (NEP) framework that accelerates training by over 100x via analytical gradients, adaptive optimization, and a two-stage training strategy, while maintaining compatibility and improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: The original NEP training strategy is computationally demanding, limiting systematic exploration of model architectures and training protocols.
AI.method: TorchNEP implements NEP in PyTorch with analytically derived gradients, adaptive optimization, and a two-stage training strategy.
AI.result: Training accelerated by >100x; improved force/stress predictions; consistent energy accuracy; two-stage training (not optimization algorithm) is key for accuracy gain; increasing model complexity does not necessarily improve prediction.
AI.conclusion: TorchNEP provides an efficient and flexible training framework for developing more accurate and robust machine-learned interatomic potentials.

title: Optimal transport of signed fractal measures with dimensional distortion: a variational characterization
comment: We extended optimal transport for signed fractal measures to controlled dimensional distortion, establishing a well-posed penalized problem with a unique map~$T^{\varepsilon}$ for~$\varepsilon \in (0, \varepsilon_{\max})$ and coupled Monge--Ampère equations. Future work will optimize~$\varepsilon$, assess window-size consistency, and test sensitivity to gradual versus abrupt dimension shifts
summary: We extend the optimal transport theory for signed measures supported on Ahlfors-regular fractal sets (Bwo'Nyahre et al., 2026) to allow a controlled dimensional distortion between source and target. A penalization term $\varepsilon Φ(d_s(x) - d_t(y))$ -- where $Φ$ is a fixed smooth strictly convex function and $d_s, d_t$ are the local Hausdorff dimensions of the fractal supports -- is added to the transport cost on inter-sign regions, with~$\varepsilon \ge 0$ controlling the tolerance for distortion. Under hypotheses H1--H7, we prove: the existence and uniqueness of an optimal transport map~$T^{\varepsilon}$ for every~$\varepsilon > 0$; coupled Monge--Ampère equations with a distortion correction term, generalizing the classical Brenier--Caffarelli equation; a double Legendre--Fenchel characterization of the optimal potentials, giving a complete variational description of the transport in each of the four sign regimes. The double Legendre--Fenchel system (Theorem~4.2) is the central contribution: it shows that the optimal potentials are the unique fixed points of a system of conjugacy equations, one per transport regime, and it provides the foundation for numerical algorithms and asymptotic analysis.

AI.tldr: Extends optimal transport theory for signed measures on fractal sets by introducing a dimensional distortion penalty, proving existence/uniqueness of optimal maps and a double Legendre-Fenchel characterization.
AI.motivation: To extend optimal transport theory from standard Euclidean settings to signed measures on Ahlfors-regular fractal sets, where source and target may have different local Hausdorff dimensions.
AI.method: Introduces a penalization term εΦ(d_s(x)-d_t(y)) in the transport cost on inter-sign regions; formulates coupled Monge-Ampère equations with distortion correction; derives a double Legendre-Fenchel system (Theorem 4.2) characterizing optimal potentials via conjugacy equations across four sign regimes.
AI.result: Proves existence and uniqueness of optimal transport map T^ε for ε>0 under hypotheses H1–H7; establishes generalized Monge-Ampère equations; provides complete variational description via double Legendre-Fenchel system.
AI.conclusion: The double Legendre-Fenchel system is the central contribution, enabling numerical algorithms and asymptotic analysis for fractal-supported signed measures with dimensional distortion.

title: Explicit Fourier Integrator for the Periodic dNLS via Gauge Transformation: Low-Regularity Estimates in Discrete Bourgain Spaces
summary: The derivative nonlinear Schrödinger equation is a fundamental model for the propagation of nonlinear dispersive waves in, for example, plasma physics and nonlinear optics. In this work, we consider this model on the one-dimensional torus and study a filtered explicit Fourier integrator for the corresponding periodic problem. After applying a periodic gauge transformation, we consider a frequency-truncated model and its filtered exponential-Euler discretization. The main difficulty comes from the derivative cubic nonlinearity in the periodic setting, since local smoothing is unavailable and resonant interactions are stronger than in the non-periodic case. To address this issue, we develop a discrete Bourgain-space framework adapted to the gauge-transformed equation. For initial data $u_0 \in H^s(\mathbb{T})$ with $1/2 < s \le 5/2$, we prove that the numerical error is of order $\mathcal{O}(τ^{s/2-1/4})$ in $H^{1/2}(\mathbb{T})$, where $τ$ denotes the employed time step size. Numerical experiments confirm the predicted convergence behavior and demonstrate the effectiveness of the filtered scheme for rough solutions.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a filtered explicit Fourier integrator for the derivative nonlinear Schrödinger equation on the torus, proving convergence rates in H^{1/2} for rough initial data using a discrete Bourgain-space framework.
AI.motivation: To address the numerical challenges posed by the derivative cubic nonlinearity in the periodic setting, where local smoothing is unavailable and resonant interactions are stronger than in the non-periodic case.
AI.method: A periodic gauge transformation is applied, followed by frequency truncation and filtered exponential-Euler discretization. A discrete Bourgain-space framework adapted to the gauge-transformed equation is developed to handle the derivative cubic nonlinearity.
AI.result: For initial data u0 in H^s(T) with 1/2 < s ≤ 5/2, the numerical error is O(τ^{s/2-1/4}) in H^{1/2}(T). Numerical experiments confirm the predicted convergence.
AI.conclusion: The filtered scheme is effective for rough solutions of the derivative NLS equation on the torus, with rigorous error bounds proven via the discrete Bourgain-space approach.

title: Once more: Leaky MHD waves in coronal magnetic flux tubes
summary: By a detailed comparison of leaky magnetohydrodynamic waves in coronal magnetic flux tubes with leaky electromagnetic waves in dielectric media it is shown that the latter kind may be called quasi-normal modes, since they can be regularised by a normalisation which systematically cuts off the contribution of the external homogeneous region, whereas such a possibility is forbidden for the former kind by the conservation of magnetic flux. Consequently, leaky magnetohydrodynamic waves cannot be systematically applied to coronal seismology, i.e. to the inverse spectral problem of determining the different equilibrium distributions of the fields by comparing the spectra they produce with the observed ones.

AI.tldr: Leaky MHD waves in coronal flux tubes cannot be regularized like leaky electromagnetic waves in dielectric media due to magnetic flux conservation, making them unsuitable for coronal seismology.
AI.motivation: To determine whether leaky magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) waves can be systematically applied to coronal seismology by comparing them to leaky electromagnetic waves in dielectric media.
AI.method: Detailed comparison between leaky MHD waves in coronal magnetic flux tubes and leaky electromagnetic waves in dielectric media, analyzing their regularization properties.
AI.result: Leaky electromagnetic waves can be regularized as quasi-normal modes by cutting off the external homogeneous region, but leaky MHD waves cannot be regularized this way due to magnetic flux conservation.
AI.conclusion: Leaky MHD waves cannot be systematically applied to coronal seismology for solving inverse spectral problems.

title: Machine-learned prediction of carbon interstitial clusters in diamond
summary: Diamond hosts optically active point defects central to quantum technologies, yet the carbon self-interstitials introduced during growth and irradiation compete with them and form new defects whose configurational landscape is poorly charted, as subtle energy differences govern the competing minima and pathways. Here we build an interstitial-focused dataset by active learning and benchmark three machine-learning interatomic potentials -- GAP, NEP and the equivariant MACE -- against density functional theory for energies, forces and migration barriers. MACE reproduces the reference energetics and relative stabilities, whereas the others can misorder the ground states. Annealing molecular dynamics with the validated potentials uncovers a series of previously unreported carbon interstitial clusters, from di- to octa-interstitials -- several introducing in-gap states of interest as colour centres -- and shows that their metastability is governed by kinetically accessible pathways rather than energetic ordering. These results chart the interstitial defect landscape and accelerate defect discovery for quantum technologies.

AI.tldr: This paper uses active learning to build a carbon interstitial dataset, benchmarks three ML potentials (GAP, NEP, MACE) against DFT, finds MACE most accurate, and uses it to discover previously unreported carbon interstitial clusters (di- to octa-interstitials) in diamond, revealing metastability is governed by kinetic pathways rather than energy ordering — with implications for quantum technologies.
AI.motivation: Carbon self-interstitials in diamond compete with optically active point defects central to quantum technologies, but their configurational landscape is poorly understood due to subtle energy differences. The authors aim to chart this landscape systematically.
AI.method: Active learning to build an interstitial-focused dataset; benchmark three ML potentials (GAP, NEP, equivariant MACE) against DFT; annealing molecular dynamics with validated potentials to explore defect clusters.
AI.result: MACE accurately reproduces DFT energetics and relative stabilities, while GAP and NEP can misorder ground states. MD simulations uncover previously unreported carbon interstitial clusters (di- to octa-interstitials), some with in-gap states relevant as colour centres. Metastability is governed by kinetically accessible pathways, not energetic ordering.
AI.conclusion: These results chart the interstitial defect landscape and accelerate defect discovery for quantum technologies.

title: Scattering for the 4D Zakharov system below the ground state
summary: For the Zakharov system in four space dimensions, we prove that all solutions inside the potential well of the ground states are global and scattering in the energy space, with no other restriction such as symmetry. The proof has already been reduced by [3] to ruling out the existence of a minimal non-scattering solution that is precompact along some trajectory. This paper carries out the final step in the proof, namely we exclude the possibility of precompact solutions inside the potential well by combining two distinct arguments depending on the motion of trajectory.

AI.tldr: Proves global existence and scattering for Zakharov system solutions in 4D within the ground state potential well, using precompactness exclusion.
AI.motivation: To establish that all solutions of the Zakharov system in four space dimensions, within the potential well of ground states, are global and scatter in energy space without symmetry restrictions.
AI.method: Builds on prior work reducing the problem to excluding a minimal non-scattering precompact solution; combines two distinct arguments based on trajectory motion to eliminate precompact solutions inside the potential well.
AI.result: Successfully rules out precompact solutions, thereby proving global existence and scattering for all solutions inside the potential well.
AI.conclusion: The Zakharov system in 4D has no minimal non-scattering precompact solutions within the potential well; all such solutions are global and scatter.

title: A Gradient Recovery Method for Electron Magnetohydrodynamics with Fractional Dissipation
summary: We propose and analyze a structure-preserving numerical method for the $2\tfrac{1}{2}$-dimensional (2.5D) electron magnetohydrodynamics system with fractional dissipation on the periodic torus. The method works directly with the magnetic field components and combines this component formulation with the gradient recovery operator of [T. Chu, H. Guo, and Z. Zhang, SIAM J. Numer. Anal., 63 (2025), pp. 23--53]. We establish discrete energy stability for a semi-implicit structure-preserving formulation and use an explicit-Hall integrating-factor implementation for efficient computation on periodic grids. The fractional dissipation is treated exactly in Fourier space, and the in-plane divergence constraint is enforced by a spectral Hodge projection. Numerical experiments demonstrate second-order spatial convergence and stable Hall-driven dynamics across several benchmark tests.

AI.tldr: A structure-preserving numerical method for 2.5D electron magnetohydrodynamics with fractional dissipation is proposed, combining component formulation, gradient recovery, semi-implicit energy stability, and spectral Hodge projection, achieving second-order convergence.
AI.motivation: To develop a structure-preserving numerical method for the 2.5D electron magnetohydrodynamics system with fractional dissipation that maintains discrete energy stability and handles fractional dissipation accurately.
AI.method: Combines component formulation of magnetic fields with gradient recovery operator; uses semi-implicit structure-preserving formulation for energy stability, explicit-Hall integrating-factor for efficiency, exact fractional dissipation treatment in Fourier space, and spectral Hodge projection for divergence constraint.
AI.result: Second-order spatial convergence and stable Hall-driven dynamics demonstrated across several benchmark tests.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is effective for simulating 2.5D electron magnetohydrodynamics with fractional dissipation, offering both accuracy and stability.

title: Caustic-Driven Fluidic Microlenses for Enhanced Nonlinear and High-Energy-Density Physics
comment: Submitted to Physical Review Applied; under review
summary: We demonstrate that caustic microlensing occurring in a liquid jet efficiently drives linear, nonlinear, and high-energy-density phenomena. In the linear regime, caustics provide localized focusing, distinct from external high-NA optics. In the nonlinear regime, they enhance the input field at the liquid-air interface and boost surface-sensitive processes. In the high-energy-density domain, caustic-driven localized laser absorption generates gigapascal shocks using microjoule femtosecond pulses, with scalability up to repetition rates of 0.2 MHz. Caustic-driven fluidic microlensing offers opportunities for surface nonlinear optics, ultrafast science, and high-energy-density physics.

AI.tldr: Caustic microlensing in liquid jets enables linear focusing, nonlinear surface enhancement, and gigapascal shock generation with microjoule femtosecond pulses at high repetition rates.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate caustic microlensing in liquid jets as a versatile platform for achieving localized focusing, nonlinear surface enhancement, and high-energy-density phenomena without external high-NA optics.
AI.method: Utilizing caustic microlensing occurring at a liquid-air interface in a liquid jet to drive linear focusing, nonlinear surface processes, and high-energy-density shock generation using microjoule femtosecond pulses.
AI.result: Caustic microlensing produces localized linear focusing distinct from high-NA optics, enhances surface nonlinear processes, and generates gigapascal shocks with microjoule pulses scalable to 0.2 MHz repetition rates.
AI.conclusion: Caustic-driven fluidic microlensing is a promising approach for applications in surface nonlinear optics, ultrafast science, and high-energy-density physics.

title: The Heat Kernel Expansion: Curvature for Shock Detection in Higher-Order Financial Networks
summary: This work follows the evolution of financial networks in Norway over a period of nine years at a monthly rate. The data consist of board directors and their affiliations to companies, which we model as simplicial complexes. In this framework, directors are represented as nodes and companies as faces of the complex. To characterize the latter, we focus on three topological measures: the Euler characteristic, computed through the Betti numbers, torsion computed through the reduced determinant of the higher-order Laplacians, and higher-order clustering coefficients. The first two fail to capture the effect of imposed law on representation, unlike our notion of curvature which is a geometrical measure computed from the coefficients of the series expansion of the heat kernel in powers of time, which is our major contribution in this work. In particular, the Euler characteristic integrates curvature, and thus local information is lost. Subsequently, not every topological measure can reliably capture shocks in networks. Further, the number of spanning trees may undergo significant changes at the lowest order, yet these changes need not be reflected in the torsion. Conversely, the change in the curvature revealed variation in the board interlock due to legislation, and serves as a sensitive measure for detecting shocks in networks. Inflection points in curvature are associated with external forcing, and minima with shock arrival times. Sharp transitions are also observed in the components of torsion, while smooth changes are observed in higher-order clustering.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the evolution of Norwegian financial networks (2009-2018) using simplicial complexes of directors and companies, proposing curvature as a sensitive geometric measure for detecting shocks (e.g., legislative changes) in networks, outperforming Euler characteristic, torsion, and higher-order clustering.
AI.motivation: To understand how financial networks evolve over time and whether topological/geometric measures can reliably detect external shocks (e.g., legislation changes) in board interlock networks.
AI.method: Models directors as nodes and companies as faces of simplicial complexes; computes Euler characteristic (via Betti numbers), torsion (via reduced determinant of higher-order Laplacians), higher-order clustering coefficients, and proposes curvature (from heat kernel series expansion) to analyze monthly network data over nine years in Norway.
AI.result: Euler characteristic and torsion fail to capture the effect of imposed law on representation. Curvature reveals variation in board interlock due to legislation, with inflection points associated with external forcing and minima with shock arrival times. Sharp transitions in torsion components, smooth changes in higher-order clustering.
AI.conclusion: Curvature serves as a sensitive measure for detecting shocks in networks, while not all topological measures reliably capture network shocks.

title: A Capacitary Approach to Semilinear Elliptic Inequalities with Potentials on Weighted Graphs
summary: We develop a capacitary approach to semilinear elliptic inequalities on weighted graphs with a potential. More precisely, we study the nonexistence of nontrivial nonnegative solutions of \[ Δu+w(x)u+v(x)u^σ\le0 \qquad\text{in }V, \] where \((V,ω,μ)\) is a connected, locally finite weighted graph, \(Δ\) is the associated graph Laplacian, \(σ>1\), \(v>0\), and \(w\) is a real-valued potential. The potential term is handled by means of a positive solution \(H\) of \(ΔH+wH=0\), which transforms the operator \(Δ+w\) into the \(H\)-Laplacian associated with a new weighted graph. Our main nonexistence criterion is formulated directly in terms of cut-off functions and the regions where their \(H\)-Laplacian is controlled. Unlike metric criteria based on pseudo-metric annuli, our formulation determines the capacitary sets from the support of the \(H\)-Laplacian estimates for the cut-off functions. We provide an example showing that our result applies in situations not covered by previous nonexistence criteria based on structural lower bounds or pseudo-metric annular volume estimates. We also show that the growth exponent in our capacitary condition is sharp by constructing an example for which the condition fails by an arbitrary power \(R^\varepsilon\), while a positive nontrivial solution exists.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a capacitary approach to study nonexistence of nontrivial nonnegative solutions to semilinear elliptic inequalities on weighted graphs with a potential, using an H-Laplacian transformation and cut-off functions.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of previous nonexistence criteria (based on pseudo-metric annuli or structural lower bounds) by formulating a more general capacitary condition using cut-off functions and H-Laplacian estimates.
AI.method: Introduces a positive solution H of ΔH + wH = 0 to transform the operator Δ + w into an H-Laplacian on a new weighted graph. Nonexistence is formulated via cut-off functions and control of their H-Laplacian on capacitary sets.
AI.result: The capacitary condition is sharp—failure by an arbitrary power R^ε still permits positive nontrivial solutions. The method applies where previous pseudo-metric or structural bounds do not.
AI.conclusion: The capacitary approach provides a more flexible and sharp nonexistence criterion for semilinear elliptic inequalities on weighted graphs with potentials.

title: Well-balanced second-order approximation of the compressible atmospheric Euler equations
summary: We introduce a second-order approximation to the compressible atmospheric Euler equations with gravity that is invariant domain preserving and well-balanced with respect to rest states. The approximation is built upon discrete auxiliary states derived from a hydrostatic reconstruction of the density. These auxiliary states, together with an affine shift of the numerical state, provide local bounds needed for maintaining well-balancing and invariant domain preserving properties of the method. The numerical method is then verified and validated with analytic solutions, well-balancing tests, and typical benchmark problems for atmospheric flows.

AI.tldr: Introduces a second-order, invariant domain preserving, well-balanced numerical method for compressible atmospheric Euler equations with gravity, using hydrostatic density reconstruction.
AI.motivation: To develop a numerical approximation for atmospheric Euler equations with gravity that simultaneously ensures invariant domain preservation and well-balancedness with respect to rest states.
AI.method: Builds a second-order approximation using discrete auxiliary states from hydrostatic density reconstruction, combined with an affine shift of the numerical state to maintain local bounds for well-balancing and invariant domain preservation.
AI.result: The method is verified and validated through analytic solutions, well-balancing tests, and standard atmospheric flow benchmark problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully achieves both invariant domain preservation and well-balancedness for atmospheric flow simulations.

title: Advancing Threshold-Inception Modeling for Predictive Simulation of Ionic Wind Fan Performance
summary: This study investigates the predictive capability of a threshold inception-based multiphysics modeling approach for ionic wind fans by direct comparison with experimental measurements. A wire-to-cylinder electroaerodynamic (EAD) fan with variable electrode spacing is used as a reference system to assess the model's ability to reproduce airflow characteristics, discharge current, and performance trends under atmospheric conditions. Numerical simulations show good qualitative agreement with experimental results across all tested configurations; however, systematic deviations emerge at higher voltages and larger electrode gaps. Analysis of these discrepancies indicates that the commonly adopted assumption of perfectly smooth emitter surfaces can limit model accuracy. Experimental characterization of the emitter wire reveals micro-scale surface protrusions, which locally enhance the electric field and alter corona inception behavior. Incorporating representative surface roughness into the numerical model improves quantitative agreement with measured airflow velocities. The results demonstrate that while the threshold inception model provides a robust foundation for EAD fan simulations, electrode surface morphology is a critical factor for reliable prediction. This work advances the validation and refinement of ionic wind fan modeling methodologies and identifies key considerations for the development of more accurate engineering-oriented simulation tools.

AI.tldr: This paper validates a threshold inception-based multiphysics model for ionic wind fans against experiments, finding that incorporating electrode surface roughness significantly improves predictive accuracy.
AI.motivation: To validate and refine multiphysics modeling approaches for ionic wind fans by direct comparison with experimental measurements, addressing the gap between simulation and real-world performance.
AI.method: A wire-to-cylinder electroaerodynamic (EAD) fan with variable electrode spacing was modeled using a threshold inception-based multiphysics approach, and results were compared with experimental data. Surface roughness was then incorporated into the model to improve accuracy.
AI.result: Numerical simulations showed good qualitative agreement but systematic deviations at higher voltages and larger gaps. The assumption of perfectly smooth emitters was identified as a key limitation; incorporating micro-scale surface roughness significantly improved quantitative velocity predictions.
AI.conclusion: The threshold inception model is a robust foundation for EAD fan simulations, but electrode surface morphology is critical for reliable prediction, highlighting the need for roughness consideration in engineering-oriented simulation tools.

title: The Cauchy-Dirichlet Problem for Complex Hessian Flows: From A Priori Estimates to Pluripotential Theory
summary:
We study the Cauchy--Dirichlet problem for parabolic complex Hessian equations on Hermitian manifolds and on bounded strictly m-pseudoconvex domains. In the smooth setting, we prove global existence and uniqueness of classical solutions under the presence of an admissible parabolic subsolution, by establishing a priori estimates up to the parabolic boundary. The estimates combine parabolic boundary techniques for complex Hessian equations with interior second order estimates and a blow-up argument.
  We then develop a general pluripotential framework for degenerate right-hand sides with L^p densities, p>n/m, and bounded Cauchy--Dirichlet data. Since the usual automorphism and Walsh-type arguments do not directly apply in a variable Hermitian background, we use approximation by smooth data, balayage, parabolic Perron envelopes, and a continuous obstacle approximation based on Harvey--Lawson--Plis subequation theory. The resulting solution is continuous for positive time, locally uniformly Lipschitz and semi-concave in time, and continuous up to the initial slice when the initial datum is continuous. We also prove a parabolic comparison principle via time regularization, Riemann sum approximations, and mixed Hessian inequalities.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes existence, uniqueness, and regularity of solutions to parabolic complex Hessian equations on Hermitian manifolds and bounded strictly m-pseudoconvex domains, covering both smooth and degenerate settings with L^p densities.
AI.motivation: To solve the Cauchy-Dirichlet problem for parabolic complex Hessian equations in both smooth and degenerate settings, extending existing theory to Hermitian manifolds (where automorphism/Walsh arguments fail) and developing a pluripotential framework for rough data.
AI.method: For smooth case: a priori estimates combining parabolic boundary techniques, interior second order estimates, and blow-up arguments. For degenerate case: approximation by smooth data, balayage, parabolic Perron envelopes, and continuous obstacle approximation based on Harvey-Lawson-Plis subequation theory.
AI.result: Global existence and uniqueness of classical solutions under admissible parabolic subsolution in smooth setting. For degenerate case: continuous solutions for positive time, locally uniformly Lipschitz and semi-concave in time, continuous up to initial slice if initial datum is continuous. Parabolic comparison principle established.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive treatment of parabolic complex Hessian equations on Hermitian manifolds, addressing both classical and weak solution theories with novel techniques that bypass limitations of previous approaches.

title: Second order explicit splitting scheme for fluid-poroelastic structure interaction problems
summary:
Efficient and provably accurate partitioned methods for fluid-poroelastic structure interaction remain challenging because explicit treatment of the Stokes-Biot interface coupling condition can compromise stability. In this work, we develop and analyze a fully discrete, second-order, explicit splitting scheme for the time-dependent Stokes-Biot problem on fixed domains. The method combines BDF2 time stepping with second-order Adams-Bashforth extrapolation of interface data through a Robin reformulation, yielding a partitioned algorithm in which the Stokes and Biot subproblems are solved independently and in parallel at each time step.
  The main analytical contribution is a rigorous stability and error analysis for this second-order explicit coupling strategy. Using BDF2 energy identities, a sharp decomposition of the extrapolated interface terms, and discrete trace estimates, we prove a closed stability bound under a parabolic CFL condition. We then derive an a priori error estimate through a projection-based framework using a Fortin projection for the fluid variables and Ritz-type projections for the poroelastic variables. The analysis identifies consistency defects from BDF2 time discretization, Adams-Bashforth interface extrapolation, and the projected kinematic relation. It shows that the total errors in fluid velocity, structure velocity, pore pressure, and elastic displacement are bounded by C times the sum of the kth power of the mesh size and the square of the time step, for k from 1 to 3, in bulk energy norms.
  Numerical experiments with manufactured solutions confirm second-order temporal convergence and optimal-order spatial convergence. We also include a moving-domain example with Navier-Stokes fluid flow, demonstrating applicability beyond the fixed-domain Stokes-Biot setting analyzed.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and analyzes a second-order explicit partitioned scheme for the Stokes-Biot problem (fluid-poroelastic structure interaction), proving stability under a CFL condition and optimal error bounds, with numerical validation including a moving-domain extension.
AI.motivation: Efficient and provably accurate partitioned methods for fluid-poroelastic structure interaction are challenging because explicit treatment of the Stokes-Biot interface coupling can compromise stability.
AI.method: The method combines BDF2 time stepping with second-order Adams-Bashforth extrapolation through a Robin reformulation, enabling parallel independent solves of Stokes and Biot subproblems. Analysis uses BDF2 energy identities, trace estimates, and projection-based framework (Fortin + Ritz-type projections).
AI.result: Rigorous stability bound under a parabolic CFL condition and a priori error estimate showing total errors bounded by C times (mesh size^k + time step^2) for k=1,...,3 in bulk energy norms. Numerical experiments confirm second-order temporal and optimal spatial convergence.
AI.conclusion: The explicit partitioned scheme is stable and accurate for the Stokes-Biot problem, and demonstrates applicability beyond fixed domains (moving-domain Navier-Stokes example).

title: A Social Force Model of the Evacuation from a Big Box Store
summary: We include elliptical cross-sections to physically represent people, and irregular polygons to represent wheelchair users, in an anisotropic social force model whose velocity and angular dependence also captures the social tendency for people to avoid walking into one another. Physical interactions are included that depend on the area of overlap between people, or obstacles, to capture normal forces that resist compression and tangential forces that resist sliding motion. The model is further extended to include decision making capabilities, small social groups, the spread of panic, and herding behavior. A large box store is simulated during an evacuation where people move through the store, along the shortest path, to their desired exits. The effects of exit choice, or the perceived availability of exits, on exit times is elucidated. It is found that ignoring 'staff only' exits, and only exiting from the main entrances, can significantly increase average egress times.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an anisotropic social force model with elliptical cross-sections for pedestrians and irregular polygons for wheelchair users, incorporating physical interactions, decision-making, social groups, panic spread, and herding behavior. Evacuation simulations of a large box store show that ignoring staff-only exits significantly increases egress times.
AI.motivation: To improve pedestrian evacuation modeling by incorporating realistic physical representations (elliptical/polygonal shapes), anisotropic social forces (avoiding walking into others), and behavioral phenomena (panic, herding, group dynamics) to better predict egress times.
AI.method: An anisotropic social force model with elliptical cross-sections for people and irregular polygons for wheelchair users, including velocity/angular dependent social forces, area-overlap-based physical interactions (normal compression and tangential sliding), decision-making, small social groups, panic spread, and herding behavior. Simulation of a large box store evacuation with shortest-path routing to exits.
AI.result: Ignoring 'staff only' exits and relying solely on main entrances significantly increases average egress times during evacuations.
AI.conclusion: Exit choice and perceived exit availability (e.g., ignoring non-obvious exits like staff-only doors) critically impact evacuation efficiency, and the model can capture these effects through realistic pedestrian dynamics and decision-making.

title: Homogenization of the compressible Navier-Stokes equations via two-scale convergence in perforated domains
summary: We study the homogenization of the compressible isentropic Navier-Stokes equations in periodically perforated domains where the size of the obstacles is of the same order as the distance between neighboring obstacles. Using the two-scale convergence method, which can be characterized via the unfolding operator, we derive the corresponding macroscopic model determined by Darcy's law. In particular, the macroscopic density satisfies the porous medium equation. The main challenge lies in identifying the pressure term in the limit. We overcome this by establishing the strong two-scale convergence of the densities, which is achieved by controlling the oscillation defect measure of the unfolded densities. A crucial contribution of our work is the development of a methodological framework applicable to more complex compressible fluid models. Furthermore, regarding conservative forces, we extend existing results from the literature to adiabatic constants $γ> \frac95$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the homogenization of compressible Navier-Stokes equations in perforated domains, deriving a macroscopic Darcy's law model and porous medium equation for density, with a new framework applicable to more complex fluids and extending results to adiabatic constants γ > 9/5.
AI.motivation: To derive a macroscopic model for compressible fluid flow in perforated domains (porous media) and overcome challenges in identifying the pressure term in the limit, while extending existing results to a wider range of adiabatic constants.
AI.method: Uses two-scale convergence method characterized via the unfolding operator, and establishes strong two-scale convergence of densities by controlling the oscillation defect measure of unfolded densities.
AI.result: Derives a macroscopic model determined by Darcy's law, where the macroscopic density satisfies the porous medium equation. Extends results to adiabatic constants γ > 9/5.
AI.conclusion: Develops a methodological framework applicable to more complex compressible fluid models and successfully extends existing results to γ > 9/5 for conservative forces.

title: Invariant measures of the stochastic theta method for stochastic differential equations with super-linearly growing coefficients
summary: The stochastic theta method is proposed to approximate invariant measures of stochastic differential equations (SDEs), both of whose drift and diffusion coefficients may grow super-linearly. For the numerical solution generated by the stochastic theta method, we show the existence and uniqueness of the numerical invariant measure first. Then, we prove that the numerical invariant measure is convergent to the exact invariant measure of the underlying SDE. We also provide some numerical simulations to illustrate our theoretical results. This work could be regarded as an extension of the results in [Y. Jiang et al, Numer. Algorithms 83(4)(2020), pp. 1531-1553] to the case of super-linearly growing diffusion coefficient. As the backward Euler-Maruyama (EM) method is a special case of the stochastic theta method, the results derived in this work could also be regarded as a generalization of the results for the backward EM method in [W. Liu et al. Appl. Numer. Math. 184(2023), pp. 137-150] to the stochastic theta method.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the stochastic theta method to approximate invariant measures of SDEs with super-linear growth in both drift and diffusion coefficients, proving existence, uniqueness, and convergence of numerical invariant measures with supporting simulations.
AI.motivation: To extend existing results on numerical invariant measures for SDEs from the case of super-linear drift only (Jiang et al. 2020) and from the backward Euler-Maruyama method (Liu et al. 2023) to the more general stochastic theta method with super-linear diffusion coefficients.
AI.method: The stochastic theta method is employed to numerically approximate invariant measures of SDEs with super-linear growth in both drift and diffusion terms. Theoretical analysis establishes existence and uniqueness of the numerical invariant measure, followed by convergence proof to the exact invariant measure.
AI.result: The existence and uniqueness of the numerical invariant measure for the stochastic theta method is established. The numerical invariant measure is shown to converge to the exact invariant measure of the underlying SDE. Numerical simulations confirm the theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully generalizes prior results to cover super-linearly growing diffusion coefficients using the stochastic theta method, unifying and extending previous work by Jiang et al. and Liu et al.

title: Physics-guided discovery of dynamical dark-energy equations of state through iterative AI reasoning
summary: Phenomenological model building has traditionally relied on human reasoning: equations are proposed from theoretical intuition, analogy, or empirical convenience, and only then tested against data. Here we show that this cycle can be recast as an iterative AI reasoning process for dynamical dark energy. Our framework uses a large language model to propose equations of state together with cosmological rationales, grounded by retrieval from the dark-energy literature and refined through autonomous evaluation. Each candidate is embedded in a cosmological model, optimized against observations, and assessed using likelihood performance and theoretical consistency. An independent language-model critic scores the physical motivation, novelty, clarity, stability and implementation validity of both the equation and its rationale, allowing subsequent proposals to evolve jointly in mathematical structure and physical reasoning. Applied to cosmological data combinations including supernovae, baryon acoustic oscillations and Planck likelihoods, the framework identifies two parameterizations that, to the best of our knowledge, have not previously been explored and that are competitive with established forms. For Pantheon+ supernovae, DESI DR2 baryon acoustic oscillations and the full Planck 2018 temperature, polarization, and lensing likelihoods, the best AI-selected model attains larger Bayesian evidence than the traditional parameterizations considered here by more than one unit. These results show that AI-guided reasoning can complement physical model building by proposing and evaluating interpretable phenomenological parameterizations for dynamical dark energy.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that AI (specifically LLMs) can iteratively propose, refine, and evaluate dynamical dark energy models, discovering two novel parameterizations that outperform traditional ones in Bayesian evidence by >1 unit.
AI.motivation: To move beyond traditional human-intuition-driven phenomenological model building by recasting it as an iterative AI reasoning process, enabling the discovery of novel, physically interpretable parameterizations for dynamical dark energy.
AI.method: An LLM proposes equations of state with cosmological rationales, grounded by retrieval from dark-energy literature and refined via autonomous evaluation. An independent LLM critic scores physical motivation, novelty, clarity, stability, and implementation validity. Candidates are embedded in cosmological models and assessed using likelihood performance and theoretical consistency.
AI.result: Two previously unexplored parameterizations were identified that are competitive with established forms. The best AI-selected model achieves larger Bayesian evidence than traditional parameterizations by more than one unit when tested against Pantheon+ supernovae, DESI DR2 BAO, and full Planck 2018 data.
AI.conclusion: AI-guided reasoning can complement human physical model building by proposing and evaluating interpretable phenomenological parameterizations for dynamical dark energy.

title: Bifurcation of overdetermined capillary problems in a strip domain

AI.tldr: This paper studies an overdetermined capillary problem in 2D, showing that when the domain is an infinite strip, a trivial 1D solution exists, and via bifurcation analysis, a critical period T* is identified where nontrivial 2D solutions branch off, existing in periodic domains with non-straight boundaries, offering physical insights into capillary phenomena.
AI.motivation: To extend the classical overdetermined capillary problem beyond trivial one-dimensional solutions and understand when genuinely two-dimensional periodic solutions can arise, providing deeper physical interpretation of capillary phenomena.
AI.method: Bifurcation argument: analyzing the existence of a critical period T* at which a branch of non-trivial solutions bifurcates from the trivial one-dimensional solution.
AI.result: Established the existence of a critical period T* where nontrivial, genuinely two-dimensional solutions bifurcate from the trivial solution, defined in unbounded periodic domains diffeomorphic to an infinite strip but with non-straight boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The bifurcation result provides significant physical interpretation for capillary phenomena, showing that periodic capollay surfaces with non-straight boundaries can emerge from the trivial flat solution at a critical period.

title: A fast direct solver based neural network for solving PDEs
summary: The matrices arising from large scale $N$-body problems can be efficiently represented using hierarchical matrices, whose key idea is that the admissible off-diagonal sub-matrices can be well approximated by low-rank matrices across a hierarchy of matrix partitions. HODLR (Hierarchical Off-Diagonal Low-Rank) matrices are a subclass of hierarchical matrices in which all off-diagonal submatrices at every level of a recursive binary partition are low-rank. In this article, we present a neural network that learns the inverse operation of HODLR matrices based on the fast direct solver for HODLR matrices developed by Ambikasaran and Darve (2013). We further extend the architecture to learn nonlinear solution operators associated with PDEs by replacing some of the linear layers with deep sub-networks. We demonstrate the performance of the proposed architecture by performing a comprehensive set of experiments that include (i) solving a linear problem such as the Fredholm integral equation of the second kind, (ii) solving PDEs such as the nonlinear Schrödinger equation, Burgers' equation, and the steady-state Darcy's flow equation, (iii) generalization study across varying parameter values, (iv) comparing the inference time of the proposed network with the run time of a classical numerical solver, and (v) comparing the proposed network with some of the existing neural operator learning networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a neural network architecture that learns the inverse operation of HODLR matrices and extends it to learn nonlinear solution operators for PDEs, demonstrating strong performance across multiple benchmark problems including Fredholm integral equations, nonlinear Schrödinger, Burgers', and Darcy flow equations.
AI.motivation: To develop a neural network that can efficiently learn matrix inverses (specifically HODLR matrices) and extend this capability to solve PDEs, bridging the gap between classical hierarchical matrix solvers and neural operator learning.
AI.method: The architecture is based on the fast direct solver for HODLR matrices by Ambikasaran and Darve (2013), and extends to nonlinear PDEs by replacing some linear layers with deep sub-networks.
AI.result: Demonstrated performance on solving Fredholm integral equations, nonlinear Schrödinger equation, Burgers' equation, and steady-state Darcy's flow, with generalization studies, inference speed comparisons, and benchmarking against existing neural operator networks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HODLR-based neural network effectively learns both linear inverse operations and nonlinear solution operators, showing promising results in accuracy, generalization, and computational efficiency compared to classical solvers and existing neural operator methods.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention
summary: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

AI.tldr: SEA-PINN introduces a squeeze-excitation attention mechanism into PINNs for dynamic neuron recalibration, achieving highly stable initialization and competitive accuracy on benchmark problems, serving as an effective lightweight plug-in module.
AI.motivation: To address instability and poor initialization in physics-informed neural networks, and to enhance nonlinear representation power without relying on Fourier features or periodic activations.
AI.method: Incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into PINNs to dynamically recalibrate neuron importance across layers, providing stable initialization.
AI.result: On 17/20 benchmarks, nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss; competitive accuracy (83% vs 90% improvement over FNN-PINN on high-frequency case 7); boosting TSA-PINN by 42.49%.
AI.conclusion: SEA-PINN is a lightweight plug-in module that enhances convergence, robustness, and reliability of physics-informed learning.

title: Stability of Vortex Patches in Channels
summary: In this paper, we investigate the orbital stability of vortex patches for the two-dimensional incompressible Euler equations in both a class of domains that satisfy the ``weak finite volume condition" and a strip of arbitrary width. We establish that for suitable parameters $(μ,λ)$, the penalized kinetic energy functional admits a minimizer, and that every such minimizer satisfies the elliptic equation $ω= λ(ψ- W x_2 - γ)_+$. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the set of minimizers is orbitally stable under the Eulerian dynamics. This work extends the variational framework developed by Abe and Choi to domains that lack both spatial scaling invariance and horizontal translation invariance. The absence of these properties introduces substantial difficulties in the proof, as classical rearrangement and scaling arguments are no longer applicable. We overcome these obstacles by comparing the Green's function with that of the half-plane and exploiting the decay condition to formulate a concentration-compactness argument that ultimately yields the desired stability result.

AI.tldr: This paper studies orbital stability of vortex patches in 2D Euler equations for domains without scaling or horizontal translation invariance, using a concentration-compactness argument.
AI.motivation: To extend the variational framework for vortex patch stability (developed by Abe and Choi) to more general domains that lack spatial scaling invariance and horizontal translation invariance.
AI.method: The authors compare the Green's function with that of the half-plane and exploit decay conditions to formulate a concentration-compactness argument, overcoming the inapplicability of classical rearrangement and scaling techniques.
AI.result: For suitable parameters (μ,λ), the penalized kinetic energy functional has a minimizer, each minimizer satisfies ω = λ(ψ - Wx₂ - γ)₊, and the set of minimizers is orbitally stable under Eulerian dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The variational framework is successfully extended to domains satisfying the "weak finite volume condition" and strips of arbitrary width, proving orbital stability of vortex patches despite the loss of symmetry properties.

title: Structure-Oriented Randomized Neural Networks for Poisson-Nernst-Planck and Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Navier-Stokes Systems
summary: We develop a structure-oriented randomized neural network framework, termed SO-RaNN, for the Poisson-Nernst-Planck (PNP) system and the Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Navier-Stokes (PNP-NS) system. The decoupled linearized subproblems are solved iteratively by randomized neural networks in a space-time framework. For the concentration variables, a pointwise cut-off is used to enforce positivity at the value level, and discrete mass-scaling factors are computed at selected correction instants and interpolated in time, so as to ensure exact mass matching at those instants and to promote approximate mass preservation between them. To introduce an auxiliary discrete dissipation mechanism, we further employ an SAV-type post-processing correction, which yields monotonicity of the SAV auxiliary variable under the ideal SAV update. For the PNP-NS system, a structure-preserving randomized neural network (SP-RaNN) is used for the velocity field, so that the velocity approximation satisfies the incompressibility constraint pointwise by construction. On the theoretical side, we derive residual-based estimates for the raw, uncorrected RaNN solvers of the linearized subproblems, formulate a conditional local-in-time convergence result for the raw outer Picard iteration of the PNP system, and analyze the value-level positivity correction together with the mass-correction and SAV post-processing steps. For the PNP-NS system, we establish an approximation result for the SP-RaNN space and provide a conditional error statement for the corresponding linearized Oseen-type problem. Numerical experiments demonstrate approximation accuracy in the source-driven manufactured tests and illustrate the intended value-level positivity correction, selected-time mass matching, computed free-energy curves based on the final gauge-fixed potential, and divergence-free approximation in benchmark tests.

AI.tldr: We present SO-RaNN, a structure-oriented randomized neural network framework for solving PNP and PNP-NS systems, incorporating positivity correction, mass preservation, SAV post-processing, and divergence-free velocity approximation, with theoretical error estimates and numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To develop a neural network-based solver for complex coupled PNP and PNP-NS systems that preserves key physical structures (positivity, mass conservation, energy dissipation, incompressibility) while maintaining computational efficiency through randomized neural networks.
AI.method: Decoupled linearized subproblems solved iteratively by randomized neural networks in space-time; pointwise cut-off for positivity; discrete mass-scaling with temporal interpolation; SAV-type post-processing for dissipation; SP-RaNN for divergence-free velocity approximation.
AI.result: Theoretical residual-based estimates for uncorrected RaNN solvers, conditional local-in-time convergence for PNP Picard iteration, positivity/mass/SAV correction analysis, and approximation results for SP-RaNN space. Numerical experiments confirm accuracy, positivity enforcement, mass matching, free-energy computation, and divergence-free approximation.
AI.conclusion: The SO-RaNN framework effectively preserves essential physical structures of PNP and PNP-NS systems while providing reliable approximations, supported by both theoretical guarantees and numerical evidence.

title: Liouville Theorem for $(p,q)$-Laplace Equations
summary: We employ the vector field method to establish a Liouville-type theorem for a class of \((p,q)\)-Laplace equations in the Euclidean space \(\mathbb{R}^n\). By modifying the exponents in the differential identity, we prove nonexistence in the subcritical range \(p-1<α<q^*-1\), where \(q^*=nq/(n-q)\). The approach relies on constructing a suitable differential identity, carrying out precise integral estimates with cutoff functions, and combining sign control and decay of the cutoff errors.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a Liouville-type theorem (nonexistence of nontrivial solutions) for \((p,q)\)-Laplace equations in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) in the subcritical range \(p-1<\alpha<q^*-1\) using the vector field method with a carefully constructed differential identity and cutoff function estimates.
