AI.motivation: To establish a Liouville-type theorem for a class of \((p,q)\)-Laplace equations in Euclidean space, specifically proving nonexistence of solutions in a certain parameter range.
AI.method: The vector field method is employed, involving: (1) constructing a suitable differential identity, (2) modifying exponents in the differential identity, (3) carrying out precise integral estimates with cutoff functions, (4) combining sign control and decay of cutoff errors.
AI.result: Nonexistence is proved in the subcritical range \(p-1<\alpha<q^*-1\), where \(q^*=nq/(n-q)\).
AI.conclusion: The vector field method successfully establishes a Liouville-type theorem for \((p,q)\)-Laplace equations under the specified subcritical range conditions.

title: A posteriori error bounds for pseudo-parabolic equations using $C_0$ semigroups
summary: A class of pseudo-parabolic partial differential equations is considered. We derive a posteriori error bounds for approximations obtained by FEMs in space and a BDF formula in time. The analysis is based on the $C_0$ semigroup theory and an adaptation of the concept of elliptic reconstruction to pseudo-parabolic problems. The analysis is complemented with numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes posteriori error bounds for FEM/BDF approximations of pseudo-parabolic PDEs using semigroup theory and elliptic reconstruction, with numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To derive rigorous a posteriori error bounds for numerical approximations of pseudo-parabolic PDEs, which are less studied than parabolic or hyperbolic problems.
AI.method: Employs C0 semigroup theory and adapts elliptic reconstruction techniques to the pseudo-parabolic setting, combined with FEM (spatial) and BDF (temporal) discretization.
AI.result: A posteriori error bounds are derived and numerically verified.
AI.conclusion: The proposed error analysis framework is effective for pseudo-parabolic problems and validated by experiments.

title: Two Flavon Froggatt-Nielsen Models with Genetic Algorithms
summary: We present the first systematic and comprehensive scan of two-flavon Froggatt-Nielsen (FN) models, employing artificial intelligence techniques to explore the high-dimensional, mixed discrete-continuous parameter space. Extending the standard single-flavon FN framework to a two-flavon setup in which separate flavon fields couple independently to the up- and down-type sectors, we demonstrate that the relative phase between their vacuum expectation values (vevs) provides a natural and generic source of CP violation absent in single-flavon models. To explore this enlarged model space, we cast the search for phenomenologically viable models as a multi-objective optimisation problem, formulating each experimental constraint as a separate objective, and employ the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm III to simultaneously fit all 18 FN charges, 45 Wilson coefficients, and flavon parameters to both the quark and lepton sectors. Our approach requires no separate training phase and identifies phenomenologically viable models orders of magnitude faster than prior reinforcement learning methods. Imposing experimental constraints on CKM and PMNS mixing angles and CP phases, charged fermion masses, and neutrino squared-mass differences, we discover over $100\,000$ unique viable models with a remarkably low duplication rate, indicating that the space of valid two-flavon FN realisations has not been exhausted. Both Normal and Inverted neutrino mass squared orderings are realised, with the relative hierarchy between the flavon vevs producing qualitatively distinct predictions for the effective neutrinoless double beta decay mass $m_{ee}$. We further demonstrate the existence of minimal FN realisations with maximal flavon exponent as small as three, and of models reproducing charged fermion masses to within $6\%$ without any dedicated continuous parameter optimisation.

AI.tldr: This paper uses AI (NSGA-III) to systematically scan two-flavon Froggatt-Nielsen models, finding over 100,000 viable models that fit quark and lepton data, with the relative phase between flavon vevs providing a natural source of CP violation.
AI.motivation: Extend single-flavon FN models to two-flavon setups to naturally accommodate CP violation, and efficiently explore the high-dimensional parameter space that traditional methods cannot handle.
AI.method: Cast model search as multi-objective optimization (18 FN charges, 45 Wilson coefficients, flavon parameters) using the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm III (NSGA-III), with each experimental constraint as a separate objective.
AI.result: Over 100,000 unique viable models identified with low duplication rate; both Normal and Inverted neutrino mass orderings realized; minimal FN realizations with flavon exponent as low as 3; charged fermion masses fit to within 6% without continuous parameter optimization.
AI.conclusion: The two-flavon FN framework is phenomenologically rich and viable, with the relative phase between vevs naturally generating CP violation. The space of valid models is large and not yet exhausted. The AI-driven approach is orders of magnitude faster than previous RL methods.

title: On weak and viscosity solutions to a nonhomogeneous mixed local-nonlocal equation
summary: This paper explores the relationship between weak and viscosity solutions to a nonhomogeneous mixed local and non-local $p$-Laplace equation in a bounded Lipschitz domain in $\mathbb{R}^N$. Under certain conditions, we derive the comparison principle for weak subsolutions and weak supersolutions to the problem. For $1<p<\infty$, we establish that continuous weak supersolutions to the problem are viscosity supersolutions, using the comparison principle. Furthermore, we show that bounded viscosity supersolutions are weak supersolutions for $p \geq 2$.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes the equivalence between weak and viscosity solutions for a mixed local/non-local p-Laplace equation in bounded Lipschitz domains, proving that continuous weak supersolutions are viscosity supersolutions (1<p<∞) and bounded viscosity supersolutions are weak supersolutions (p≥2), via a comparison principle.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between two fundamental solution concepts (weak and viscosity) for mixed local and non-local p-Laplace equations, which is important for understanding the regularity and well-posedness of these problems.
AI.method: The authors derive a comparison principle for weak sub/supersolutions, then use it to show that continuous weak supersolutions are viscosity supersolutions for all 1<p<∞, and conversely that bounded viscosity supersolutions are weak supersolutions for p≥2.
AI.result: (1) Comparison principle for weak subsolutions and supersolutions is established. (2) Continuous weak supersolutions → viscosity supersolutions for 1<p<∞. (3) Bounded viscosity supersolutions → weak supersolutions for p≥2.
AI.conclusion: The equivalence between weak and viscosity solutions holds under specific conditions, but is asymmetric in p: the viscosity-to-weak direction requires p≥2, while the weak-to-viscosity direction holds for all p>1.

title: A conservative adaptive rank method for the Wigner-Poisson system
summary: We propose a conservative adaptive rank method for the 1D1V Wigner-Poisson system. The method targets a central challenge in deterministic quantum kinetic simulations: reducing the cost of phase-space evolution while preserving the macroscopic invariants needed for physical fidelity. The scheme combines a sampling-based adaptive rank Wigner-Poisson update [7] with a conservative macroscopic correction. A conservative density-momentum solve provides local macroscopic updates, a Fermi-Dirac-type reconstruction transfers them to the kinetic solution, and a global quadratic moment correction enforces the discrete total energy constraint at the kinetic level. Unlike Maxwell-Boltzmann-type corrections commonly used in classical kinetic settings, the reconstruction uses a Fermi-Dirac-type form motivated by the model's quantum-statistical structure. The corrected state is incorporated into an ACA SVD representation, allowing the numerical rank to adapt to the phase-space complexity generated by the nonlocal Wigner operator and self-consistent Poisson field. Numerical experiments for the two-stream instability, strong Landau damping, and bump-on tail instability show that the method captures benchmark Wigner-Poisson dynamics for several values of the quantum parameter H, maintains bounded adaptive ranks, and preserves the specified global discrete invariants with conservation errors near machine precision. We also compare this formulation, which uses local density-momentum correction plus global total energy correction, with a related globally conservative formulation for mass, momentum, and energy [8]. The two approaches produce nearly identical phase-space and diagnostic results for the periodic benchmark test considered here, indicating that both correction strategies are compatible with adaptive rank compression for Wigner-Poisson dynamics in the tested 1D1V periodic setting.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a conservative adaptive rank method for 1D1V Wigner-Poisson simulations that reduces phase-space computational cost while preserving mass, momentum, and energy invariants. It combines sampling-based adaptive rank updates with a Fermi-Dirac-type macroscopic correction and ACA SVD representation. Numerical tests show near-machine-precision conservation and bounded adaptive ranks for benchmark instability problems.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost of deterministic quantum kinetic simulations (Wigner-Poisson system) while preserving macroscopic invariants (mass, momentum, energy) required for physical fidelity.
AI.method: The method combines: (1) sampling-based adaptive rank Wigner-Poisson update, (2) conservative density-momentum solve for local macroscopic updates, (3) Fermi-Dirac-type reconstruction (motivated by quantum-statistical structure) to transfer updates to kinetic level, (4) global quadratic moment correction for total energy, and (5) ACA SVD representation for adaptive rank compression.
AI.result: Numerical experiments (two-stream instability, strong Landau damping, bump-on-tail instability) show the method captures benchmark dynamics for various quantum parameter H values, maintains bounded adaptive ranks, and preserves global invariants with errors near machine precision. The approach produces nearly identical results to a globally conservative formulation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed local density-momentum plus global energy correction strategy is compatible with adaptive rank compression for 1D1V periodic Wigner-Poisson dynamics, offering a cost-effective alternative to fully global conservative corrections while achieving equivalent accuracy.

title: Can DFT-trained neural network potentials reproduce structure, solvation, and water-exchange properties in aqueous magnesium solutions?
summary: Magnesium ions play an essential role in many biological processes but remain challenging to model in biomolecular simulations. Despite considerable scientific effort, classical force fields fail to simultaneously reproduce key structural, thermodynamic and kinetic solution properties, likely due to their inability to explicitly account for quantum many-body effects. Here, we develop and systematically benchmark MACE neural network potentials (NNPs) for aqueous MgCl$_2$ solutions trained on revPBE-D3/zd and revPBE0-D3/zd density functional theory reference data and assess their ability to reproduce a broad range of experimental solution properties including the structure of the first hydration shell, diffusion coefficient, activity derivative, water-exchange rate and mechanism as well as solvation free energy. Both NNPs accurately reproduce the octahedral structure of the first hydration shell, ion pairing properties and diffusion coefficients. Combining the NNPs with transition path sampling and other enhanced sampling techniques allows us to capture the rare event of water exchange in the first hydration shell of Mg$^{2+}$ revealing a dissociative exchange mechanism. Transition interface sampling yields exchange rates within one order of magnitude of experiment, representing a substantial improvement over classical dissociative force fields. In contrast, the NNP-derived solvation free energy significantly underestimates the experimental value, revealing a limitation of the present local NNP architectures for describing ion solvation thermodynamics. Our results demonstrate that DFT-trained NNPs can accurately describe Mg$^{2+}$ hydration structure, diffusion, ion pairing, and exchange kinetics, while highlighting the need for explicit long-range electrostatic treatments to achieve quantitative agreement with experimental ion solvation free energies.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and benchmarks MACE neural network potentials (NNPs) for MgCl₂ solutions trained on DFT reference data, showing they accurately reproduce hydration structure, diffusion, ion pairing, and water-exchange kinetics, but fail to correctly predict solvation free energy due to missing long-range electrostatic effects.
AI.motivation: Magnesium ions are crucial in biology but classical force fields fail to simultaneously reproduce structural, thermodynamic, and kinetic solution properties because they cannot explicitly account for quantum many-body effects.
AI.method: The authors develop MACE neural network potentials trained on revPBE-D3/zd and revPBE0-D3/zd DFT data, then combine them with transition path sampling and transition interface sampling to study Mg²⁺ hydration properties.
AI.result: Both NNPs accurately reproduce first hydration shell structure (octahedral), ion pairing, diffusion coefficients, and water-exchange rates (within one order of magnitude of experiment), but the solvation free energy is significantly underestimated.
AI.conclusion: DFT-trained NNPs can accurately describe Mg²⁺ hydration structure, diffusion, ion pairing, and exchange kinetics, but explicit long-range electrostatic treatments are needed for quantitative agreement with experimental solvation free energies.

title: Solutions of the 3D inhomogeneous incompressible Navier-Stokes system with initial velocity in $VMO^{-1}$
summary: In this paper, we establish local existence of strong solutions for the three-dimensional inhomogeneous incompressible Navier-Stokes equations with initial data $(ρ_0,u_0)$ lying in $C^1 \times (L^2 \cap VMO^{-1})$, where $ρ_0$ has a positive lower bound. Furthermore, if $ρ_0 \in C^2$ and $||ρ_0-1||_{L^\infty}+||u_0||_{BMO^{-1}}$ is sufficiently small, we prove global existence of the solution. To achieve this, we employ an estimate for the transport equation to obtain regularity for the density and apply a new freezing-coefficient method for the momentum equation.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes local strong solutions for 3D inhomogeneous Navier-Stokes equations with initial data in C¹ × (L² ∩ VMO⁻¹) and positive lower-bounded density, and proves global existence under smallness conditions using transport equation estimates and a freezing-coefficient method.
AI.motivation: To extend the well-posedness theory for the 3D inhomogeneous incompressible Navier-Stokes equations to rougher initial data settings (VMO⁻¹ for velocity) and to establish global existence under small perturbations.
AI.method: Uses estimates for the transport equation to improve density regularity and develops a new freezing-coefficient method for the momentum equation to handle the velocity.
AI.result: Proves local existence of strong solutions for (ρ₀,u₀) ∈ C¹ × (L² ∩ VMO⁻¹) with ρ₀ bounded below; proves global existence when additionally ρ₀ ∈ C² and ||ρ₀-1||_{L^∞}+||u₀||_{BMO⁻¹} is sufficiently small.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods (transport estimates + freezing-coefficient) successfully handle rough initial data and yield both local and global well-posedness results for the 3D inhomogeneous Navier-Stokes system.

title: Data dependent Shepard approximation through and adaptive modification of the shape parameter
summary: In this article, we introduce a novel data-dependent Shepard interpolation method inspired by the adaptive strategies proposed in [2]. In this case, as Shepard interpolation does not produce oscillations, our approach has the core objective of reducing the smearing near jump discontinuities in the data in one and two dimensions. While the original work in [2] focuses in on Radial Basis Function (RBF) interpolation, we extend these ideas to the Shepard framework by incorporating a data-dependent adaptation mechanism. Specifically, we modify the classical Shepard interpolation by adaptively adjusting the influence weights based on local smoothness indicators that modify the shape parameter. These indicators, similar to those used in [2], are designed to detect discontinuities: for grid-based data, we use squared undivided second-order differences, and for scattered data, we employ squared least-squares approximations of the Laplacian scaled by the square of the mean local separation of stencil points. The resulting data-dependent weighting scheme forces the kernels close to a discontinuity to behave like a local delta function, effectively reducing the smearing of the discontinuities introduced by the classical Shepard approach. We establish the theoretical foundation of the method, including the properties of the new interpolation and we theoretically prove that the reduction of the smearing of discontinuities is possible. Numerical experiments in one and two dimensions confirm that the proposed data-dependent Shepard interpolation significantly reduces the smearing of jump discontinuities while maintaining high accuracy in smooth regions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a data-dependent Shepard interpolation method that adaptively adjusts influence weights using local smoothness indicators to reduce smearing near jump discontinuities, with theoretical proof and numerical validation in 1D and 2D.
AI.motivation: To reduce the smearing effect near jump discontinuities in classical Shepard interpolation by incorporating adaptive strategies inspired by RBF interpolation methods.
AI.method: Modifies classical Shepard interpolation by adaptively adjusting influence weights via local smoothness indicators (squared undivided second-order differences for grid data; squared least-squares Laplacian approximations scaled by mean local separation for scattered data), making kernels near discontinuities behave like local delta functions.
AI.result: Numerical experiments in 1D and 2D confirm significant reduction of smearing near jump discontinuities while maintaining high accuracy in smooth regions.
AI.conclusion: The data-dependent Shepard interpolation effectively preserves discontinuities and is theoretically grounded, extending adaptive RBF ideas to the Shepard framework.

title: Optimizing resource allocation for accuracy in noisy variational quantum algorithms
summary: For quantum algorithms to achieve their full potential, we need methodologies to optimize them, such as reaching a given output accuracy with minimal resource costs. Here, we develop such a methodology for a class of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) algorithms. We leverage simulations of a Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) to propose a phenomenological model of such algorithms that captures the complex relationship between algorithmic accuracy, algorithmic resource costs, and the noise that exists in realistic quantum hardware. For this, we take the algorithmic resource cost to be the total number of quantum gate-operations in the algorithm; minimizing this cost typically makes the algorithm faster and more energy-efficient. We consider the subtle trade-off between quantum circuit size (small circuits are too imprecise, but large ones are too noisy), and the number of iterations of that quantum circuit for the full algorithm to sufficiently converge. Using a noise-metric-resource methodology, we identify the sweet spot (of circuit size versus iterations) that minimizes the algorithmic resource costs for a desired algorithm accuracy. It also gives the circuit size that maximizes algorithm accuracy for a fixed resource cost. Our methodology provides a practical guideline for near-term deployment of variational algorithms on realistic noisy hardware, including hardware that uses error mitigation.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a methodology for optimizing NISQ variational algorithms by modeling the trade-off between quantum circuit size and number of iterations to minimize resource costs (total gate operations) for a desired accuracy, identifying the sweet spot for deployment on noisy hardware.
AI.motivation: To enable quantum algorithms to achieve their full potential, we need optimization methodologies that minimize resource costs (e.g., gate operations) while reaching a given output accuracy, especially for NISQ-era variational algorithms.
AI.method: The authors use simulations of a Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) to build a phenomenological model capturing the relationship between algorithmic accuracy, resource costs (total gate operations), and hardware noise. They analyze the trade-off between circuit size (small circuits are imprecise, large circuits are noisy) and the number of iterations, then apply a noise-metric-resource methodology to find the optimal balance.
AI.result: The methodology identifies the sweet spot of circuit size versus iterations that minimizes algorithmic resource costs for a desired accuracy, and also the circuit size that maximizes accuracy for a fixed resource cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodology provides a practical guideline for deploying variational algorithms on near-term noisy quantum hardware, including systems that use error mitigation techniques.

title: Existence of solutions for elliptic problems involving the $(1,q)$-Laplacian operator and a discontinuous superlinear nonlinearity
summary: In this paper, we study a class of quasilinear elliptic problems involving the $(1,q)-$Laplacian operator and a discontinuous superlinear nonlinearity governed by the Heaviside function. The main difficulty of the problem arises from the presence of the $1$-Laplacian operator, whose natural setting is the Space of Functions of Bounded Variation. Our approach is based on an approximation method involving $(p,q)-$Laplacian problems as $p\to1^+$. As a consequence, we prove the existence of a nontrivial and nonnegative solution belonging to $W^{1,p}_0(Ω)$, in an appropriate weak sense. Moreover, we investigate the asymptotic behavior of the solutions as $β\to0^+$, showing that the family of solutions converges to a solution of the limit problem without discontinuity.

AI.tldr: This paper studies quasilinear elliptic problems with $(1,q)$-Laplacian and a discontinuous nonlinearity via approximation by $(p,q)$-Laplacian as $p\to1^+$, proving existence of nontrivial nonnegative solutions in $W^{1,p}_0(Ω)$ and analyzing asymptotic behavior as $β\to0^+$.
AI.motivation: To overcome difficulties from the $1$-Laplacian operator (which requires BV space) and discontinuous nonlinearity (governed by Heaviside function) in solving quasilinear elliptic problems.
AI.method: Approximation method using $(p,q)$-Laplacian problems as $p\to1^+$.
AI.result: Existence of a nontrivial, nonnegative weak solution in $W^{1,p}_0(Ω)$; convergence of solution family to the limit problem without discontinuity as $β\to0^+$.
AI.conclusion: The approximation approach successfully handles the $1$-Laplacian operator and discontinuous nonlinearity, yielding valid solutions and characterizing their asymptotic behavior.

title: Nonlinear Geotechnical Analysis Using a Polygonal Cell-Based Smoothed Finite Element Framework
summary: Nonlinear geotechnical analysis often involves complex geometries, staged construction, local failure, and mesh-dependent stress and plastic strain responses. This study develops a polygonal cell-based smoothed finite element method (CS-FEM) for nonlinear geotechnical analysis and implements it in ABAQUS through the user element subroutine. The proposed method combines Wachspress interpolation with cell-based strain smoothing, in which the smoothed strain--displacement matrix is evaluated by boundary integration over polygonal smoothing subcells. This formulation avoids direct calculation of shape-function derivatives inside polygonal elements and enables standard polygonal meshes and hybrid quadtree meshes with hanging nodes to be handled in a unified framework. Nonlinear geomaterial behavior is incorporated through incremental elasto-plastic constitutive updates, including the Mohr--Coulomb model and the Duncan--Chang model. Several benchmark and engineering examples, including a perforated plate, strip footing, core rockfill dam, tunnel excavation, and slope stability problems, are presented for verification. The results show that the proposed method accurately predicts displacement, stress, plastic strain, bearing capacity, and factor of safety, while providing improved mesh flexibility and computational efficiency for nonlinear geotechnical analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a polygonal cell-based smoothed finite element method (CS-FEM) for nonlinear geotechnical analysis, implemented in ABAQUS via user element subroutine. It combines Wachspress interpolation with cell-based strain smoothing to handle complex meshes and nonlinear soil models (Mohr-Coulomb, Duncan-Chang), demonstrating improved accuracy and efficiency through benchmarks like strip footing, dam, tunnel, and slope problems.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear geotechnical analysis faces challenges with complex geometries, staged construction, local failure, and mesh-dependent stress/strain responses. Existing FEM approaches struggle with mesh flexibility and computational efficiency in these scenarios.
AI.method: Polygonal CS-FEM combining Wachspress interpolation with cell-based strain smoothing, where the smoothed strain-displacement matrix is computed via boundary integration over polygonal subcells. Implemented in ABAQUS via user element subroutine, handling standard polygonal and hybrid quadtree meshes with hanging nodes. Nonlinear behavior captured through incremental elasto-plastic updates (Mohr-Coulomb and Duncan-Chang models).
AI.result: The method accurately predicts displacement, stress, plastic strain, bearing capacity, and factor of safety across benchmarks (perforated plate, strip footing, core rockfill dam, tunnel excavation, slope stability), with improved mesh flexibility and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed polygonal CS-FEM provides a unified, efficient, and flexible framework for nonlinear geotechnical analysis, avoiding shape-function derivative calculations and accommodating complex mesh configurations.

title: Multi-objective design of photon blockade for bright single-photon sources
summary: High-quality single-photon sources, realized through saturable emitters, photon blockade, or heralded pair generation, are indispensable building blocks for photonic quantum platforms. Although these mechanisms suppress multiphoton emission through distinct principles typically captured by analytical models, their practical implementation is constrained by conflicting requirements for purity, brightness, and indistinguishability, which must be balanced within high-dimensional design landscapes. Here, we propose a computational framework for optimizing competing metrics of single-photon sources. Building on a Liouville-space adjoint formulation that efficiently evaluates multiple objectives in Markovian open quantum systems, we develop a Jacobian-based update, which ensures first-order monotonic reduction of multi-objective costs. By incorporating simulated annealing to escape gradient-vanishing plateaus, our framework achieves a design success rate of nearly 60 % for photon blockade with g2(0) smaller than 0.1 and theoretically bounded brightness across a broad parameter space, without any analytical guidance. This framework provides a general recipe for multi-objective design of open quantum systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computational framework using Liouville-space adjoint formulation and Jacobian-based updates with simulated annealing to optimize competing metrics (purity, brightness, indistinguishability) in single-photon sources, achieving ~60% design success rate for photon blockade with g2(0)<0.1.
AI.motivation: Current single-photon source designs face conflicting requirements between purity, brightness, and indistinguishability, and existing analytical models cannot effectively navigate the high-dimensional design landscapes needed to balance these competing metrics.
AI.method: The framework combines a Liouville-space adjoint formulation for efficient multi-objective evaluation in Markovian open quantum systems, a Jacobian-based update ensuring first-order monotonic cost reduction, and simulated annealing to escape gradient-vanishing plateaus.
AI.result: The framework achieves nearly 60% design success rate for photon blockade with g2(0) smaller than 0.1 and theoretically bounded brightness across a broad parameter space, without requiring any analytical guidance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a general recipe for multi-objective design of open quantum systems, enabling automated optimization of single-photon sources that balances purity, brightness, and indistinguishability.

title: Generalized Morrey-Campanato estimates for elliptic equations with coefficients of integrable oscillation
summary: This work concerns regularity properties of weak solutions to elliptic equations in divergence form -div(a$\nabla$u) = div F , under low regularity assumptions on both the coefficient a and the source term F . We introduce generalized Morrey and Campanato spaces extending the classical definitions by replacing uniform boundedness requirements with suitable integrability conditions. Within this framework, we establish regularity estimates for the gradient of weak solutions in these generalized spaces. As applications, we recover classical H{ö}lder and Lebesgue estimates and derive fractional Sobolev regularity results. In particular, the proposed approach yields fractional Sobolev estimates in situations where the coefficient may be discontinuous and the gradient of the solution is not expected to be locally bounded.

AI.tldr: This paper develops generalized Morrey and Campanato spaces to establish gradient regularity estimates for weak solutions of elliptic equations with low-regularity coefficients and source terms, recovering classical Hölder/Lebesgue estimates and deriving fractional Sobolev results even when solutions are not locally bounded.
AI.motivation: To extend classical regularity theory for elliptic equations to settings where coefficients may be discontinuous and solution gradients are not locally bounded, by introducing more flexible function spaces.
AI.method: Introduces generalized Morrey and Campanato spaces that replace uniform boundedness with integrability conditions, then uses these spaces to prove gradient regularity estimates for weak solutions of -div(a∇u) = div F.
AI.result: Establishes regularity estimates in the new generalized spaces; recovers classical Hölder and Lebesgue estimates; derives fractional Sobolev regularity results even for discontinuous coefficients and unbounded gradients.
AI.conclusion: The generalized space approach provides a unified framework that simultaneously recovers classical results and extends regularity theory to previously inaccessible low-regularity regimes.

title: CoarseSolvers for Exascale Solution of Poisson Problems
summary: WepresentatwolevelSchwarzmethodasanalternativetoAlgebraicMultigridmethod(AMG) used as the last level (coarse) solver of the p-multigrid pMG preconditioner for pressure Poission equation resulting from Spectral/Finite element descretization of incompressible Navier-Stokes eqaution. Proposed Schwarz method consits of a local problem in the original pMG coarse space and a global coarse problem. Main contribution of the paper is a novel, structured and a non-nested coarse space for the global coarse problem. Structured nature of the proposed global coarse space enable communication-free interpolation between the original p-multgrid coarse space and the global coarse problem. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared to the state of the art AMG solver BoomerAMG by a series of experiments performed using Nek5000/RS, a suite of highly scalable incompressible Navier-Stokes solvers, on Summit/Frontier supercomputers at Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a two-level Schwarz method as a coarse solver alternative to AMG in p-multigrid preconditioners for pressure Poisson equations, featuring a novel structured non-nested coarse space that enables communication-free interpolation, and demonstrates superior performance over BoomerAMG on Nek5000/RS simulations using Summit/Frontier supercomputers.
AI.motivation: To develop a more efficient alternative to Algebraic Multigrid (AMG) as the coarse-level solver in p-multigrid preconditioners for pressure Poisson equations arising from incompressible Navier-Stokes simulations.
AI.method: A two-level Schwarz method combining a local problem in the original pMG coarse space with a global coarse problem. The key novelty is a structured, non-nested coarse space that allows communication-free interpolation between the p-multigrid coarse space and the global coarse problem.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art AMG solver (BoomerAMG) when tested on Nek5000/RS solvers running on Summit and Frontier supercomputers at Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility.
AI.conclusion: The structured non-nested coarse space Schwarz method is an effective and communication-efficient alternative to AMG for coarse-level solving in p-multigrid preconditioners, demonstrating superiority on large-scale supercomputing platforms.

title: Large spin splitting at ferromagnetic surfaces of bulk antiferromagnets
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures without appendix. To be submitted to Physical Review Letters
summary: We use density functional theory and model Hamiltonians to reveal large spin splitting of bands localized at low-symmetry, ferromagnetic surfaces of bulk antiferromagnets (AFMs). There is great interest in finding new material platforms combining the robustness and ultrafast dynamics of AFMs with large, functional spin splitting which is often restricted to ferromagnets. Here, we show that a subset of AFM surfaces which have symmetry-allowed magnetization can host large spin splitting via bulk degeneracy lifting of sublattice-resolved exchange splittings. Using model Hamiltonians, we show that the spin splitting is maximized for two ferromagnetic surface motifs: terminations with single uncompensated magnetic sublattices, and two-sublattice surfaces whose sublattices are magnetically and electronically compensated in the bulk, but acquire distinct crystal field environments via surface truncation. The latter case can yield FM-like spin splitting magnitudes while also having vanishingly small uncompensated magnetization. In contrast, when surface magnetization arises from relativistic canting on symmetry-connected sublattices, the spin splitting is expected to be small. We confirm these predictions with first-principles calculations of $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ and $\mathrm{FeF_2}$, finding splittings from $\sim10\mathrm{meV}$-$\sim1\mathrm{eV}$ depending on the surface in question. Our findings point to intrinsic surface symmetry breaking as a route to large, functional spin splitting in an expanded range of AFM materials.

AI.tldr: This paper uses DFT and model Hamiltonians to show that certain surfaces of antiferromagnets can exhibit large spin splitting (up to ~1 eV) due to symmetry breaking, offering a new route to functional spin splitting in AFM materials without requiring ferromagnetism.
AI.motivation: There is strong interest in combining the robustness and ultrafast dynamics of antiferromagnets with large spin splitting—a property typically restricted to ferromagnets. The authors seek to identify whether AFM surfaces can host such large splitting without relying on relativistic effects.
AI.method: The authors combine density functional theory (DFT) with model Hamiltonians to analyze spin splitting at low-symmetry ferromagnetic surfaces of bulk antiferromagnets. They identify two key surface motifs and validate predictions via first-principles calculations on Cr₂O₃ and FeF₂.
AI.result: Large spin splitting (from ~10 meV to ~1 eV) is found at specific AFM surfaces. Two motifs maximize splitting: (1) single uncompensated magnetic sublattice terminations, and (2) two-sublattice surfaces with distinct crystal fields via truncation. The latter yields FM-like splitting with vanishing net magnetization. Relativistic canting produces only small splitting.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic surface symmetry breaking in antiferromagnets provides a viable route to achieve large, functional spin splitting, expanding the range of AFM materials suitable for spintronic applications.

title: Spectral stability in the modified Camassa-Holm equation
comment: periodic waves in the modified Camassa-Holm equation
summary: We investigate the spectral stability of small-amplitude, periodic, traveling-wave solutions of the modified Camassa-Holm equation with cubic nonlinearities. More precisely, we analyze the $L^2(\mr)$-spectrum of the associated linearized operator in a neighborhood of the origin in the spectral plane. Inspired by a recently novel method based on Kato's perturbation theory [Berti et al, Full description of Benjamin-Feir instability of Stokes waves in deep water, \textit{Invent. Math.}, 230 (2022), 651-711.], we provide a complete description of the spectrum near the origin of the linearized operator--an integro-differential operator with periodic coefficients--and thus prove that such waves are not subject to modulational instability. Moreover, a spectral analysis reveals a remarkable threshold phenomenon: such waves with wave number $k^2\leq 3$ exhibit spectral stability, while instability emerges when $k^2>3$.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes spectral stability of small-amplitude periodic traveling waves of the modified Camassa-Holm equation with cubic nonlinearities, showing no modulational instability but a threshold phenomenon at k²=3.
AI.motivation: To understand whether periodic traveling-wave solutions of the modified Camassa-Holm equation are subject to modulational (Benjamin-Feir type) instability, and to characterize the spectrum of the linearized operator near the origin.
AI.method: The authors use Kato's perturbation theory, inspired by Berti et al.'s recent work, to analyze the L²(ℝ)-spectrum of the linearized integro-differential operator with periodic coefficients near the origin of the spectral plane.
AI.result: Small-amplitude waves are not subject to modulational instability. A threshold phenomenon is discovered: waves with wavenumber k² ≤ 3 are spectrally stable, while instability occurs when k² > 3.
AI.conclusion: The modified Camassa-Holm equation exhibits a stability threshold at k²=3, with spectral stability below this value and instability above it, despite the absence of modulational instability.

title: Computational Identifiability
summary: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions. In this paper, we explore a fundamental distinction between this theoretical, idealized notion of identifiability and a proposed alternative that is computation-bound. The framework we propose - 'computational identifiability' - is to instead define a finite computational search procedure for an empirical estimator. If this process finds an estimator empirically, within a desired error tolerance, then identifiability is satisfied, conditional on the specified assumptions of the search (i.e., a prior distribution over the parameters) and conditional on the search procedure itself. Through several experiments, we demonstrate how this framework allows us to answer fine-grained, practical identification questions, such as identification with small finite samples, with ambiguous graphical criteria, with mixed observational-interventional data, and across counterfactual data and estimands. Code is available at https://github.com/lbynum/metadentify.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "computational identifiability," a finite search-based framework for empirical causal effect estimation that relaxes the asymptotic assumptions of traditional theoretical identifiability, enabling practical identification with small samples, ambiguous graphs, mixed data types, and counterfactual queries.
AI.motivation: Traditional causal identification relies on asymptotic, infinite-data assumptions that don't hold in practice. The authors aim to bridge the gap between theoretical identifiability and real-world finite-sample settings where practitioners need actionable answers despite imperfect information.
AI.method: The authors define a finite computational search procedure that empirically finds an estimator within a desired error tolerance, conditional on specified prior distributions and search procedures. This replaces the mathematical uniqueness proof of traditional identifiability with an empirical search process.
AI.result: The framework is demonstrated across several experiments: identification with small finite samples, ambiguous graphical criteria, mixed observational-interventional data, and counterfactual data and estimands.
AI.conclusion: Computational identifiability offers a practical alternative to theoretical identifiability, allowing researchers to answer fine-grained identification questions under realistic data constraints that traditional asymptotic theory cannot address.

title: Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs
summary: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

AI.tldr: QCPIKAN is a novel quantum-classical hybrid framework using Chebyshev-KAN layers and quantum circuits to solve PDEs with exponential error convergence, outperforming existing methods in porous media flow problems.
AI.motivation: To develop a more efficient and accurate hybrid quantum-classical framework for solving PDEs, addressing limitations of existing physics-informed neural networks in error convergence and numerical dispersion.
AI.method: Combines Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers with parameterized quantum circuits in a hybrid architecture, embedding physical constraints into the training loss function for physical consistency.
AI.result: Superior performance over existing quantum-classical PINNs across three seepage scenarios (single-phase flow, component transport, two-phase flow) in global accuracy, local error control, dynamic tracking, and displacement front localization.
AI.conclusion: QCPIKAN offers a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs, with theoretical guarantees of exponential high-frequency error convergence and mitigated numerical dispersion.

title: Minimizers for Coulomb gases constrained to a halfspace
summary: We consider a family of optimization problems, based on a mean-field description of particles interacting through Coulomb forces in a quadratic trap. In addition, the particles are constrained to lie in a halfspace and we are interested in the way the particle distribution changes as the halfspace varies. In particular, we can prove the existence of a phase transition, thereby settling a recent conjecture by Byun, Forrester, Majumdar and Schehr.

AI.tldr: This paper studies optimization problems for Coulomb-interacting particles in a quadratic trap constrained to a halfspace, proving a phase transition that confirms a recent conjecture.
AI.motivation: To understand how particle distribution changes under halfspace constraints and to settle a conjecture by Byun, Forrester, Majumdar, and Schehr.
AI.method: Mean-field optimization framework for Coulomb-interacting particles with halfspace constraints; analysis of particle distribution as a function of halfspace variation.
AI.result: Proved the existence of a phase transition in the particle distribution as the halfspace varies.
AI.conclusion: The conjecture regarding the phase transition is confirmed, providing new insights into constrained Coulomb systems.

title: Learning universal approximations for partial differential equations with Physics-Informed Broad Learning System
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a central role in modeling complex physical, biological, and engineering systems. While traditional numerical solvers are robust, they often incur prohibitive computational costs due to mesh dependencies, whereas recent Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but frequently suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a novel backpropagation-free framework that reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. We improved an algorithm within this framework to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently and provide a rigorous mathematical proof establishing the universal approximation property of PIBLS for these equations. Experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs demonstrate that PIBLS is one to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs while achieving significantly higher solution accuracy. This framework provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a backpropagation-free framework that solves PDEs via direct least-squares optimization. It is 1-3 orders of magnitude faster than traditional PINNs with higher accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional PDE solvers are computationally expensive due to mesh dependencies, while PINNs suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. There is a need for a fast, stable, mesh-free alternative.
AI.method: PIBLS reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization (backpropagation-free). An improved algorithm handles nonlinear PDEs, with a mathematical proof of universal approximation property.
AI.result: PIBLS achieves 1-3 orders of magnitude faster training than conventional PINNs while obtaining significantly higher solution accuracy on both linear and nonlinear PDEs.
AI.conclusion: PIBLS provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization.

title: Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks
summary: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

AI.tldr: ASYS is an agent-driven framework that combines symbolic search with gradient-based optimization to automatically discover interpretable mathematical structures (e.g., PDE solutions) without requiring handcrafted analysis, numerical meshes, or neural networks.
AI.motivation: Mathematicians understand PDEs through mathematical structures, but traditional methods (analysis, numerical simulation, neural networks) do not directly produce such structures—they require manual effort or produce opaque outputs.
AI.method: ASYS (Agentic Symbolic Search) uses an agent guided by PDE theory and accumulated experience to generate differentiable symbolic programs, refined by evolutionary search for discrete forms and gradient-based optimization for continuous parameters.
AI.result: Across five PDE problems (bounded dynamics, blow-up, free-boundary focusing), ASYS recovers known analytical forms and discovers new interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel blow-up.
AI.conclusion: ASYS demonstrates a new paradigm for automated discovery of interpretable PDE solution structures, bridging the gap between analytical, numerical, and neural approaches.

title: Acceleration methods for the planar 3D ILSA hydraulic fracturing model
summary: Planar 3D models of hydraulic fracturing provide a practical balance between models with restrictive geometric assumptions and fully 3D simulators, capturing fractures with arbitrary planar footprints at moderate computational cost. Nevertheless, applications such as treatment design optimization and mini-frac test interpretation require large ensembles of simulations, for which the cost of planar 3D models remains a significant bottleneck. This work presents acceleration strategies for the planar 3D Implicit Level Set Algorithm (ILSA) to reduce simulation runtime while preserving numerical accuracy. A unified planar 3D ILSA scheme that consolidates the nested loops of the elastohydrodynamic solver and the front tracking algorithm into a single iterative process is introduced. A matrix splitting approach is applied to the linearized elastohydrodynamic system, moving the dense part of the elasticity operator to the right-hand side and yielding a sparse system matrix that can be solved more efficiently. Anderson acceleration is incorporated into the solution of the elastohydrodynamic system to improve convergence under varying fracture geometry. Additionally, a predictor--corrector scheme is examined with the proposed methods to assess their combined effect. Each technique is evaluated individually and in combination on both the reference and unified planar 3D ILSA schemes across five benchmark cases. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the unified scheme alone delivers an average 2.5x speedup, reaching 5.7x for the sandglass geometry. The combined application of all techniques achieves an average 4x speedup and up to 11x for the sandglass case, with the relative discrepancy in fracture aperture below 5% compared with the reference scheme.

AI.tldr: This paper presents acceleration strategies for the planar 3D Implicit Level Set Algorithm (ILSA) for hydraulic fracturing simulation, achieving up to 11x speedup while maintaining accuracy within 5% error. Key techniques include a unified iterative scheme, matrix splitting, Anderson acceleration, and predictor-corrector methods.
AI.motivation: Applications like treatment design optimization and mini-frac test interpretation require large ensembles of hydraulic fracturing simulations. The cost of existing planar 3D models remains a significant bottleneck for such large-scale studies.
AI.method: Four acceleration techniques are introduced: (1) a unified planar 3D ILSA scheme consolidating nested loops into a single iterative process; (2) matrix splitting to yield a sparse system matrix; (3) Anderson acceleration for improved convergence; (4) a predictor-corrector scheme. These are evaluated individually and combined on five benchmark cases.
AI.result: The unified scheme alone achieves an average 2.5x speedup (up to 5.7x). Combined techniques achieve an average 4x speedup and up to 11x for the sandglass case, with fracture aperture discrepancy below 5% compared to the reference scheme.
AI.conclusion: The proposed acceleration strategies significantly reduce computational cost of planar 3D hydraulic fracturing simulations while preserving numerical accuracy, making them practical for optimization and interpretation workflows.

title: Optimal dimension-dependent $\ell^p$ and $\ell^{1,\infty}$ estimates of the discrete Riesz Transforms
summary:
In this paper, we are concerned with the optimal dimension-dependent $\ell^p$ norm of the discrete Riesz Transforms $R_{\text{dis}}^{(k)}$ on $\mathbb{Z}^d$ given by the singular convolution kernel $K_k(m)=c_d m_k/|m|^{d+1}$, where $c_d=Γ(\frac{d+1}{2})/π^{(d+1)/2}$ .
  We show that for fixed $1<p<\infty$, when $d\to \infty$ $$\|R_{dis}^{\left( k \right)}\|_{\ell ^p\left( \mathbb{Z}^d \right) \rightarrow \ell ^p\left( \mathbb{Z}^d \right)}=2c_d\left( 1+\frac{\left( \sqrt{2}+o\left( 1 \right) \right) d}{2^{\frac{d}{2}}} \right) .$$ The operator norm of $R_{\text{dis}}^{(k)}$ grows super-exponentially as $d\to\infty$ since $c_d\sim(\frac{d-1}{2eπ})^{\frac{d-1}{2}}\sqrt{\frac{d-1}π}$ by Stirling's formula, which gives a negative answer to the conjecture proposed by Bañuelos, Kim and Kwaśnicki in \cite{BKK}. The optimal dimension-dependent $\ell^{1,\infty}$ estimate of $R_{\text{dis}}^{(k)}$ is also established.

AI.tldr: This paper disproves a conjecture by Bañuelos, Kim, and Kwaśnicki by showing that the operator norm of discrete Riesz Transforms on ℤ^d grows super-exponentially as dimension d→∞, with explicit asymptotic formulas for ℓ^p and ℓ^{1,∞} estimates.
AI.motivation: To determine the optimal dimension-dependent ℓ^p norm of discrete Riesz Transforms and settle a conjecture about their growth behavior in high dimensions.
AI.method: Asymptotic analysis of the operator norm ‖R_dis^(k)‖ using Stirling's formula and dimension-dependent estimates, examining the singular convolution kernel K_k(m)=c_d m_k/|m|^{d+1}.
AI.result: For fixed 1<p<∞, as d→∞, the operator norm = 2c_d(1 + (√2+o(1))d/2^{d/2}), which grows super-exponentially since c_d ~ ((d-1)/(2eπ))^{(d-1)/2} √((d-1)/π). Also establishes optimal ℓ^{1,∞} estimate.
AI.conclusion: The super-exponential growth gives a negative answer to the conjecture by Bañuelos, Kim and Kwaśnicki.

title: Randomized Sketching is Robust to Low-Precision Rounding on GPUs
summary:
Randomized sketching is a core primitive in randomized numerical linear algebra. On modern hardware architectures, in particular on GPUs, the performance of sparse sketches is limited by memory traffic and atomic accumulation rather than floating-point throughput. This makes sketching a natural target for mixed precision, provided that low-precision accumulation does not degrade the embedding quality.
  We study mixed-precision GPU implementations of sparse oblivious subspace embeddings, focusing on a SparseStack generalization of the GPU CountSketch kernel of Higgins et al. SparseStack improves embedding quality relative to CountSketch on coherent inputs, but its additional nonzeros per column increase atomic-update contention and reduce throughput. We therefore implement FP16 SparseStack variants using deterministic round-to-nearest, exact stochastic rounding, and dithered rounding, and compare them with FP32 SparseStack, CountSketch, mixed-precision CountSketch, and FlashSketch.
  Our main empirical finding is that, for the tested regimes, SparseStack embedding quality is insensitive to the FP16 rounding rule. Deterministic, stochastic, and dithered rounding FP16 SparseStack produce nearly identical subspace distortion and sketch-and-solve least-squares accuracy across incoherent, coherent, and adversarial test problems. The dominant accuracy factor is the sketch distribution rather than the quantization rule: SparseStack variants substantially improve distortion on coherent inputs, while all methods behave similarly on incoherent inputs. Since deterministic rounding has the lowest overhead, it provides the best performance--accuracy tradeoff among the FP16 SparseStack variants.

AI.tldr: This paper studies mixed-precision GPU implementations of sparse randomized sketches, finding that FP16 SparseStack embedding quality is insensitive to rounding rule (deterministic, stochastic, dithered), and that sketch distribution matters more than quantization for accuracy.
AI.motivation: Randomized sketching on GPUs is bottlenecked by memory traffic and atomic operations, not compute. This makes it a natural candidate for mixed-precision acceleration, but it's unclear whether low-precision accumulation degrades embedding quality.
AI.method: The authors implement FP16 SparseStack variants with three rounding rules (deterministic round-to-nearest, exact stochastic rounding, dithered rounding) and compare them against FP32 SparseStack, CountSketch, mixed-precision CountSketch, and FlashSketch on GPU hardware.
AI.result: FP16 SparseStack embedding quality is nearly identical across all three rounding rules for subspace distortion and least-squares accuracy. The dominant factor is sketch distribution (SparseStack vs. CountSketch) rather than quantization precision or rounding method.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic rounding provides the best performance-accuracy tradeoff among FP16 SparseStack variants, and SparseStack substantially improves distortion on coherent inputs while all methods behave similarly on incoherent inputs.

title: Sparse Configuration Interaction for the Electronic Schrödinger Equation Revisited: Complete Basis Set Limit Complexity and Quantum-Encoding Impact
summary: In this article we revisit regularity results for eigenfunctions in the discrete spectrum of the electronic Schrödinger equation and study their consequences for approximation complexity. In particular, for the convergence to the complete basis set limit, it can be shown that the curse of dimensionality in the leading algebraic exponent can be mitigated. That is, for general sparse grid constructions, the main term of the convergence rate with respect to the number of degrees of freedom is independent of the number of electrons. These insights indicate potential benefits for classical numerical solvers of the electronic Schrödinger equation and also for quantum-computing approaches through new qubit-efficient wavefunction encodings.

AI.tldr: This paper revisits eigenfunction regularity in the electronic Schrödinger equation, showing the curse of dimensionality in convergence rates can be mitigated for sparse grid methods, benefiting both classical and quantum computing approaches.
AI.motivation: To analyze how eigenfunction regularity affects approximation complexity in solving the electronic Schrödinger equation, particularly regarding the curse of dimensionality.
AI.method: Revisits regularity results for eigenfunctions in the discrete spectrum and studies their consequences for convergence to the complete basis set limit using sparse grid constructions.
AI.result: The main term of the convergence rate with respect to degrees of freedom becomes independent of the number of electrons, mitigating the curse of dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: These insights benefit classical numerical solvers and enable new qubit-efficient wavefunction encodings for quantum computing.

title: Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: Equal advising: Daria Pugacheva and Fedor Ratnikov. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI and PDEs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a two-stage evolutionary algorithm approach to optimize hyperparameters for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), improving training stability and solution accuracy under fixed computational budgets.
AI.motivation: PINNs suffer from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and sensitivity to hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex physics-informed loss. Traditional hyperparameter optimization methods like gradient-based strategies get trapped in suboptimal regions.
AI.method: A two-stage evolutionary approach: Stage 1 performs low-fidelity training runs (truncated epochs) to rapidly screen candidate hyperparameter configurations as a black-box optimization problem. Stage 2 fully trains only the most promising candidates using standard gradient-based optimizers.
AI.result: Evaluated on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz equations, the method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary algorithms provide a robust mechanism for PINNs hyperparameter optimization by combining exploration (evolutionary search) and exploitation (gradient-based refinement), improving both accuracy and robustness under fixed budgets.
